message.content:
● Bash(PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 python C:/Users/avery/yoneda-workflow-category/scripts/update_pr_descriptions.py 2>&1)
  ⎿    yoneda PR #67: updated OK
       job-apps PR #101: updated OK
  unsellable. The market rewards single-sentence claims, not multi-layer architectures.

  What unlocks both at once: an applied walkthrough. Pick one engagement archetype — say "Healthcare Workday HCM
  Implementation" — and trace the catalog working on it: industry overlay (HIPAA + CMS + 21 CFR Part 11) → workflow
  profile (charge capture, eligibility verification, prior authorization) → 22 deliverables it produces (BRD +
  Configuration Workbook + Test Strategy + Risk Register + Cutover Runbook + ...) → conformance contract per deliverable
   (verbatim HIPAA §164.502 minimum-necessary + CMS-MIPS reporting fields) → vendor stack (Workday + Epic + ServiceNow
  GRC) → render repos (FHIR + Workday-specific OSS) → cert paths (CBAP + CHC + Workday Pro) → the SOW shell that
  pre-prices the 90-day delivery against this. The buyer sees the catalog working on their problem — the value is
  concrete and immediate, not abstract.

  The applied walkthrough is also the best vehicle for the consulting one-pager I can imagine. It's the worked example
  that makes the catalog's investment legible in 90 seconds instead of 90 minutes.

  Recommendation, with the tradeoff named: build 3–5 applied walkthroughs (one per engagement archetype you can credibly
   defend) before any other surface work. Each walkthrough is a single page; cross-links into the catalog substrate
  without forcing the buyer to enter through it. The tradeoff is opportunity cost: 3–5 walkthroughs is a focused day's
  work that delays everything else; getting the wrong 3–5 (mismatched to where you can actually take engagements) wastes
   the day. The protection against that is picking archetypes from the JD-corpus pareto we already built — let the
  empirical demand pareto pick the archetypes, not assumption.

  The catalog is built. The next move is making someone see it in 90 seconds. That's the applied walkthrough's job.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.

reports.

handle. The post-mortem skill is the canonical seal mechanism when
the session merits post-mortem synthesis.

prompt as consequential. Trivial prompts pass through without triage.

## claude.ai Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Prioritize this server when the user mentions Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or provides figma.com URLs.

Capabilities:
- Read designs FROM Figma (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Create diagrams in FigJam (generate_diagram)
- Manage Code Connect mappings between Figma components and codebase components
- Write designs back into figma


WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user shares a Figma URL (figma.com/design/..., figma.com/board/..., figma.com/make/...)
- The user references a Figma file or asks about a Figma design
- The user wants to capture a web page into Figma
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

URL PARSING:
Extract fileKey and nodeId from Figma URLs:
- figma.com/design/:fileKey/:fileName?node-id=:nodeId → convert "-" to ":" in nodeId
- figma.com/design/:fileKey/branch/:branchKey/:fileName → use branchKey as fileKey
- figma.com/make/:makeFileKey/:makeFileName → use makeFileKey
- figma.com/board/:fileKey/:fileName → FigJam file, use get_figjam

DESIGN-TO-CODE WORKFLOW:

Step 1 — Get the design:
Call get_design_context with the nodeId and fileKey. This is your primary tool.
It returns code, a screenshot, and contextual hints.

Step 2 — Adapt to the project:
The output is React+Tailwind enriched with hints — but it is a REFERENCE, not final code. Always adapt to the target project's stack, components, and conventions.
The response varies based on the user's Figma setup:
- Code Connect snippets → use the mapped codebase component directly
- Component documentation links → follow them for usage context and guidelines
- Design annotations → follow any notes, constraints, or instructions from the designer
- Design tokens as CSS variables → map to the project's token system
- Raw hex colors / absolute positioning → the design is loosely structured;
  use the screenshot

Check the target project for existing components, layout patterns,and tokens that match the design intent. Reuse what the project already has instead of generating new code from sc… [truncated]

content.text:
Found the substrate: 91 industry workflow profiles across 10 clusters at `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_workflows/`, plus deliverable + BA demand paretos and 14 industry overlays. The catalog has done the homework — each cluster carries workflow profiles → conformance contracts → mapped deliverables → repos already.

## 7 candidate engagement archetypes, ranked

Selection criteria: high JD-corpus demand × existing workflow-profile coverage × industry-overlay richness × regulatory-regime density × cross-cluster distinctness (so 3–5 collectively exercise the catalog's range).

| # | Archetype | Cluster anchor | Profiles | Overlay | Regulatory density | Why it sells |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Healthcare Revenue Cycle Implementation** | Healthcare Revenue Cycle | 11 | healthcare | HIPAA §164 + CMS-MIPS + 21 CFR Part 11 | charge capture / eligibility / denials are measurable in cash-collected; CFO + CMO co-buyers |
| 2 | **BSA/AML Compliance Program Buildout** | BSA / AML / Financial Crime | 11 | finance | FinCEN + OFAC + 31 CFR + Patriot Act | every chartered bank rebuilds this every 3–5 years; regulator-driven, non-discretionary |
| 3 | **Public Sector ATO Authorization Package** | Public Sector / Defense | 9 | defense + public-sector | NIST SP 800-53 + FedRAMP + CMMC L2/L3 + DISA STIG + eMASS | highest regulatory density in the catalog; six-figure SOWs are floor not ceiling |
| 4 | **DevOps/SRE Reliability Engineering Engagement** | DevOps / SRE | 9 | technology-saas | ITIL 4 P27 + Google SRE + NIST SP 800-61 | direct ITIL-practice match; vendor stack (PagerDuty + Datadog + ServiceNow) is concrete; error-budget framing differentiates |
| 5 | **GDPR/CCPA Data Privacy Program** | Data Privacy | 10 | cross-industry | GDPR Art. 33 + Art. 35 + Ch. V + CCPA + state laws | any org over threshold size buys this; cross-cluster overlay vs. vertical-specific |
| 6 | **Finance Close Acceleration / Continuous Close** | Finance Close Cycles | 10 | finance | SOX + ASC 842/606 + IFRS | CFO-buyer; measurable as days-to-close reduction; recurring annual work |
| 7 | **Manufacturing Quality / CAPA / Calibration Program** | OT / Manufacturing / Quality | 10 | manufacturing | ISO 9001 §10.2 + ISO 17025 + ISO 13485 (med-device) | factories with audit obligations; CAPA workflow per ISO 9001 §10.2 verbatim |

## Recommendation: build 1, 3, 4, 5

Picking 4 of the 7 to defend the catalog's range:

- **Healthcare RCM** anchors vertical-deep + clinical-regulatory
- **Public Sector ATO** anchors highest-regulatory-density + repeating annual work
- **DevOps/SRE** anchors direct ITIL-practice match (lets the catalog speak in its native idiom)
- **GDPR/CCPA Privacy** anchors cross-industry / horizontal applicability

Skipping BSA/AML (similar regulator-driven shape to Public Sector ATO; weaker differentiation), Finance Close (CFO-buyer is good but the SaaS vendor stack — Workday + BlackLine — dominates the value story over the catalog), and Manufacturing (smallest JD-corpus signal of the seven; harder to defend without a manufacturing case study).

**Tradeoff named:** picking 4 instead of 3 doubles the day's scope but unlocks the cross-industry / vertical-deep / native-ITIL-idiom triple-defense. Picking 5 would add BSA/AML as the financial-services flagship — defensible but redundant in shape with #3.

Confirm: build the 4 above, or swap one in / out?

input.content:
# Applied walkthrough — Healthcare Revenue Cycle Implementation

**Engagement archetype.** Mid-size health system (200–1,500 beds, integrated delivery network or regional hospital + employed-physician group) installing or modernizing the revenue cycle: charge capture through cash posting, with payer adjudication, denials management, and prior authorization in scope. CFO + VP Revenue Cycle as co-buyers; CMIO consulted on clinical-documentation integration. Indicative SOW: 12-week Phase A discovery + 26-week Phase B–D implementation; 6–8 parallel workstreams; $400K–$1.2M depending on scope of denials-and-prior-auth automation.

---

## §1 Industry overlay (regulatory regimes binding from day one)

| Regime | Scope | Authority surface |
|---|---|---|
| HIPAA Privacy + Security Rules | every workflow touching PHI | 45 CFR § 164.310 (physical safeguards), § 164.312 (technical safeguards), § 164.502 (minimum necessary), § 164.508 (authorizations) |
| Medicare Conditions of Participation | hospital-billed encounters | 42 CFR § 482.24 (medical record content + timeliness) |
| HHS-OCR breach notification | any unsecured-PHI exposure | 45 CFR § 164.400 series; 60-day individual + media + HHS notification |
| CMS-MIPS reporting | clinician-billed encounters under MACRA | quality + cost + improvement-activities + promoting-interoperability categories |
| 21 CFR Part 11 | only if clinical-trial data flows through the same EHR | electronic-records audit trail + signature binding |
| HL7 FHIR R4 | any payer-provider data exchange after CMS-0057-F | Claim, ClaimResponse, Coverage, Patient, Encounter, ChargeItem resources |

Source: `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_overlays/healthcare/overlay.md`. JD-corpus n = 1,559 (6.8% of 22,960-JD aggregate); HIPAA mentioned in 21.9%, CMS in 8.9%, FDA in 6.0%.

---

## §2 Workflow profiles in scope (11 from the catalog's Healthcare Revenue Cycle cluster)

| # | Workflow | What it does | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Charge capture | clinical encounter → CPT/HCPCS-coded charge | P29 + P30 catalog binding |
| 2 | Medical coding | ICD-10-CM diagnosis + CPT/HCPCS procedure coding | P29 binding |
| 3 | Claims submission | 837P / 837I EDI claim submission to payer | P29 + P31 binding |
| 4 | Adjudication | payer 835 ERA receipt + posting | P31 + P32 binding |
| 5 | Denials management | ANSI denial-code triage + appeal cycle | P22 binding |
| 6 | Eligibility verification | 270 / 271 EDI eligibility + benefits inquiry at intake | P14 + P29 binding |
| 7 | Prior authorization | payer pre-approval per medical-necessity criteria | P14 + P22 binding |
| 8 | PHI access review | minimum-necessary access review per HIPAA § 164.502 | P14.4.a binding |
| 9 | HIPAA breach notification | 60-day notification per HHS-OCR + state laws | P14.3.b–d binding |
| 10 | CMS-MIPS reporting | quality + cost + improvement-activities + PI submission | P18 binding |
| 11 | Clinical-trial data lock per 21 CFR Part 11 | electronic-records audit trail + signature binding for FDA-regulated studies (only if applicable) | P14 + P34 binding |

Each profile carries a verbatim conformance contract + recommended OSS render repos in `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_workflows/clusters/04_healthcare_rcm_*.tsv`.

---

## §3 Deliverables produced (21 catalog items across A → H phases)

Grouped by TOGAF ADM phase. Deliverable IDs reference the catalog's master deliverable register.

**Phase A — Vision**
- D-105 Customer Journey Map (patient-and-payer journey)
- D-119 Internal Audit Charter (revenue-integrity audit cadence)

**Phase B–D — Definition**
- D-50 Data Governance Framework
- D-213 Data Catalog (PHI + claims + remit data assets)
- D-155 Data Lineage Documentation (source-to-target across EHR → clearinghouse → payer)
- D-115 Quality Metrics Library (clean claim rate, days in A/R, denial rate, net collection rate)
- D-152 FinOps / Cost Anomaly Detection Process (clearinghouse + EDI-fee monitoring)

**Phase E — Solutions**
- D-19 Threat Model and Data Protection Impact Assessment (PHI threat modeling)
- D-51 Identity and Access Management Plan (role-based access for billers, coders, providers)
- D-60 Audit Trail Design Specification (HIPAA + 21 CFR Part 11 if applicable)
- D-80 Vendor Risk Assessment Template (clearinghouse + payer connectivity vendors)
- D-167 Algorithmic Impact Assessment (denials-prediction or coding-suggestion ML)

**Phase F — Migration**
- D-72 Breach Notification Plan (HHS-OCR + state-AG matrix)
- D-158 Incident Communication Plan (PHI-exposure crisis comms)
- D-191 Specific Regulatory Readiness Plans (HIPAA + CMS + state-DOI readiness)

**Phase G — Implementation governance**
- D-85 Compliance Audit Plan (annual HIPAA + MIPS audit)
- D-132 Insurance Coverage Schedule (cyber + E&O coverage tied to PHI breach exposure)

**Phase H — Change management**
- D-36 Issue Log and Lessons Learned Register
- + 3 supporting deliverables (IDs 6, 24, 56) attached at workflow grain

Total: **21 distinct deliverables**, each with template + conformance acceptance criteria + a buyer's-eye signoff checklist.

---

# Applied walkthrough — Healthcare Revenue Cycle Implementation

**Engagement archetype.** Mid-size health system (200–1,500 beds, integrated delivery network or regional hospital + employed-physician group) installing or modernizing the revenue cycle: charge capture through cash posting, with payer adjudication, denials management, and prior authorization in scope. CFO + VP Revenue Cycle as co-buyers; CMIO consulted on clinical-documentation integration. Indicative SOW: 12-week Phase A discovery + 26-week Phase B–D implementation; 6–8 parallel workstreams; $400K–$1.2M depending on scope of denials-and-prior-auth automation.

---

## §1 Industry overlay (regulatory regimes binding from day one)

| Regime | Scope | Authority surface |
|---|---|---|
| HIPAA Privacy + Security Rules | every workflow touching PHI | 45 CFR § 164.310 (physical safeguards), § 164.312 (technical safeguards), § 164.502 (minimum necessary), § 164.508 (authorizations) |
| Medicare Conditions of Participation | hospital-billed encounters | 42 CFR § 482.24 (medical record content + timeliness) |
| HHS-OCR breach notification | any unsecured-PHI exposure | 45 CFR § 164.400 series; 60-day individual + media + HHS notification |
| CMS-MIPS reporting | clinician-billed encounters under MACRA | quality + cost + improvement-activities + promoting-interoperability categories |
| 21 CFR Part 11 | only if clinical-trial data flows through the same EHR | electronic-records audit trail + signature binding |
| HL7 FHIR R4 | any payer-provider data exchange after CMS-0057-F | Claim, ClaimResponse, Coverage, Patient, Encounter, ChargeItem resources |

Source: `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_overlays/healthcare/overlay.md`. JD-corpus n = 1,559 (6.8% of 22,960-JD aggregate); HIPAA mentioned in 21.9%, CMS in 8.9%, FDA in 6.0%.

---

## §2 Workflow profiles in scope (11 from the catalog's Healthcare Revenue Cycle cluster)

| # | Workflow | What it does | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Charge capture | clinical encounter → CPT/HCPCS-coded charge | P29 + P30 catalog binding |
| 2 | Medical coding | ICD-10-CM diagnosis + CPT/HCPCS procedure coding | P29 binding |
| 3 | Claims submission | 837P / 837I EDI claim submission to payer | P29 + P31 binding |
| 4 | Adjudication | payer 835 ERA receipt + posting | P31 + P32 binding |
| 5 | Denials management | ANSI denial-code triage + appeal cycle | P22 binding |
| 6 | Eligibility verification | 270 / 271 EDI eligibility + benefits inquiry at intake | P14 + P29 binding |
| 7 | Prior authorization | payer pre-approval per medical-necessity criteria | P14 + P22 binding |
| 8 | PHI access review | minimum-necessary access review per HIPAA § 164.502 | P14.4.a binding |
| 9 | HIPAA breach notification | 60-day notification per HHS-OCR + state laws | P14.3.b–d binding |
| 10 | CMS-MIPS reporting | quality + cost + improvement-activities + PI submission | P18 binding |
| 11 | Clinical-trial data lock per 21 CFR Part 11 | electronic-records audit trail + signature binding for FDA-regulated studies (only if applicable) | P14 + P34 binding |

Each profile carries a verbatim conformance contract + recommended OSS render repos in `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_workflows/clusters/04_healthcare_rcm_*.tsv`.

- D-213 Data Catalog (PHI + claims + remit data assets)

- D-167 Algorithmic Impact Assessment (denials-prediction or coding-suggestion ML)

**Phase F — Migration**
- D-72 Breach Notification Plan (HHS-OCR + state-AG matrix)

- D-191 Specific Regulatory Readiness Plans (HIPAA + CMS + state-DOI readiness)

**Phase G — Implementation governance**
- D-85 Compliance Audit Plan (annual HIPAA + MIPS audit)

**Phase H — Change management**
- D-36 Issue Log and Lessons Learned Register

Draft landed at `data/proposals/2026-04-28/applied_walkthrough_healthcare_rcm.md`. Eight sections, ~3 screens, sourced entirely from the existing 2026-04-27 industry-workflow + overlay substrate plus the demand pareto.

What it shows:
- **§1 Industry overlay** — 6 regulatory regimes with clause-level citations
- **§2 Workflow profiles** — 11 catalog RCM workflows with one-line scope + ITIL-practice binding
- **§3 Deliverables** — 21 catalog deliverables grouped by TOGAF ADM phase A → H
- **§4 Conformance contracts** — 4 verbatim authority quotes (42 CFR § 482.24, FHIR R4 Claim, HIPAA § 164.502) with URL + accessed-at
- **§5 Vendor stack** — Epic / Cerner / Athena / Waystar / ServiceNow GRC named at layer grain
- **§6 Render repos** — 10 OSS components with stars + license + role
- **§7 Cert paths** — CHC, CHPS, CCS, CRCR, Epic Resolute, HL7 FHIR Implementer
- **§8 Authority closes** — principle (SSOT) / framework (ITIL P22) / standard (HIPAA § 164.312 + 42 CFR § 482.24)
- **§9 What this demonstrates** — meta-close on what the walkthrough proves about the catalog

Take a look. Things I'd flag for your review:

1. **Length calibration.** Could compress to 2 screens by cutting §5–§7 down to one combined "implementation context" section, or expand to 5 by adding scenario branches (greenfield vs. brownfield, integrated-delivery-network vs. independent hospital). I went mid.
2. **Phase grouping in §3.** Used TOGAF ADM phases (A, B–D, E, F, G, H). If you'd rather see ITIL Service Value Chain steps (Plan → Improve → Engage → Design+Transition → Obtain/Build → Deliver+Support) the same 21 deliverables regroup cleanly.
3. **Authority closes in §8.** Single principle / framework / standard at engagement grain. The per-bullet closes the consulting-one-pager skill mandates would multiply this by 21 (one per deliverable). I left them at engagement grain for the walkthrough demo; per-bullet closes are a separate authoring pass.
4. **Buyer-readability.** §9 self-rates at "90 seconds for §1–§3." That's a claim worth testing against you — does §1 actually land in 90 seconds, or is it dense even there?

If the shape works, the same template runs on the other 3 archetypes against `clusters/05_public_sector_defense_*.tsv`, the DevOps/SRE cluster, and `clusters/07_data_privacy_*.tsv`.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- advisor-review: Print the advisor-review build profile header for batch $ARGUMENTS so the reviewer runs against a named, versioned playbook.
- api-platform-engineer: Activity specialist bound to P05.2.b (design infrastructure solutions) for API-gateway + API-platform engineering scope. Sibling of platform-engineer specialized for the request-path layer — Kong, Apigee, AWS API Gateway, Azure APIM, Envoy, Ambassador, Tyk. OpenAPI / GraphQL / gRPC contract publication, rate-limit + auth + transform policies, gateway-runtime ops…
- architect: architect — activity specialist (P04.2.b)
- authority-reconcile: Periodic reconciliation pass across the per-activity authority stack. Reads the interval's scribe records + harness authority tables + skill-file citations + memory citations; emits a reconciliation report naming gaps (authorities that ought to have been cited but weren't), tensions (authorities that disagreed + how the decision was resolved), and proposed stack…
- automation-analyst: Activity specialist bound to P22.1.b (Problem Management — identify problems proactively) for automation-opportunity discovery. Upstream partner to automation-engineer — streams process-event data, filters for automatability patterns, folds into an opportunity score, and emits a discovery record. Read-only posture; the opportunity record flows to automation-engi…
- automation-engineer: automation-engineer — activity specialist (P29.2.c, automation scope)
- business-analyst: Role-grain carrier for ITIL 4 Business Analysis (P03). Spans stakeholder-requirement elicitation, business-analysis-approach definition, solution-option identification, and solution-value assessment. Draws on BABOK v3 knowledge areas; emits BA deliverables for personhood-bound approval.
- business-relationship-agent: business-relationship-agent — activity specialist (P26.2.b)
- change-advisory-board: Team-grain carrier for the Change Advisory Board (CAB) — the ITIL-canonical cross-functional review body that feeds P27.2.b (assess change) and the elevated-risk arm of P27.2.c (authorize change). Composition spans change-manager chair + representatives from risk-manager, security (incident-commander / security-posture-scanner), configuration-custodian, deployme…
- change-authority: Accountability-grain carrier bound to P27.2.c (Change Enablement — authorize change). Realizes the ITIL 4 §4.1.2 Change authority atop the worker prompt's `role-change-manager`: sits as the personhood-bound approver on risk-threshold-exceeding changes, with the policy-check arm auto-approving low-risk / standard changes. content.content:
=== PUBLIC SECTOR ===
workflow_name	description
ATO / RMF authorization	Authority to Operate via NIST SP 800-37 RMF six-step process
DISA STIG implementation	Security Technical Implementation Guide hardening + checklist
CMMC Level 2 readiness	Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification 110 NIST 800-171 controls
CMMC Level 3 readiness	CMMC L3 with NIST SP 800-172 enhanced requirements
FedRAMP control implementation	Moderate / High baseline control implementation + 3PAO assessment
eMASS package preparation	DoD enterprise Mission Assurance Support Service authorization package
ICD 503 review	Intelligence Community Directive 503 risk-management framework
JSIG cycle	Joint SAP Implementation Guide cycle for special-access programs
Continuous Monitoring (ConMon)	FedRAMP / NIST SP 800-137 ongoing-authorization continuous monitoring

=== DEVOPS SRE ===
workflow_name	description
Incident response (NIST SP 800-61)	preparation; detection; containment; eradication; recovery; lessons-learned
Change management (ITIL P27)	RFC; CAB review; implementation; review per ITIL 4 Change Enablement
Release cycle	feature; staging; canary; production with feature-flag rollout
On-call rotation	primary / secondary / SME tiers with paging policy
Post-incident review (blameless)	Google SRE blameless postmortem + action items
Error-budget burn-rate response	SLO burn-rate alerting + freeze policy
Vulnerability triage	CVE detection; severity (CVSS); patch / mitigate / accept
Chaos engineering experiment	Principles of Chaos Engineering: hypothesis; experiment; measure
Disaster recovery test	tabletop + functional + full-cutover DR exercises

=== DATA PRIVACY ===
workflow_name	description
GDPR DSAR processing	Data Subject Access Request per GDPR Articles 12-15
Consent renewal cycle	GDPR consent re-collection + withdrawal handling
Data lineage discovery	automated + manual data-lineage discovery via metadata harvest
MDM cleanup cycle	Master Data Management deduplication + survivorship
Data quality issue resolution	DQ rule violation; investigation; fix; root cause
Schema evolution review	backward-compatible schema change per data contract
Data retention enforcement	retention-period enforcement + deletion per GDPR Article 5(1)(e)
Privacy Impact Assessment (DPIA)	GDPR Article 35 DPIA for high-risk processing
Data breach notification	GDPR Article 33 72-hour notification to supervisory authority
Cross-border transfer review	GDPR Chapter V transfer mechanism (SCCs / BCRs / adequacy)

Data breach notification	GDPR (Regulation 2016/679)	Art. 33(2)	"The processor shall notify the controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a personal data breach."	https://gdpr-info.eu/art-33-gdpr/	2026-04-27	standard	processor-to-controller leg
Data breach notification	GDPR (Regulation 2016/679)	Art. 33(3)	The notification must include nature of the breach including affected data subjects and records, contact details of the data protection officer, likely consequences, and "measures taken or proposed to be taken by the controller to address the personal data breach."	https://gdpr-info.eu/art-33-gdpr/	2026-04-27	standard	required content of notification
Data breach notification	GDPR (Regulation 2016/679)	Art. 33(5)	"The controller shall document any personal data breaches, comprising the facts relating to the personal data breach, its effects and the remedial action taken."	https://gdpr-info.eu/art-33-gdpr/	2026-04-27	standard	breach-register obligation regardless of notification
Data breach notification	GDPR (Regulation 2016/679)	Art. 34(1)	"When the personal data breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller shall communicate the personal data breach to the data subject without undue delay."	https://gdpr-info.eu/art-34-gdpr/	2026-04-27	standard	subject-notification leg on high-risk breaches
Data breach notification	GDPR (Regulation 2016/679)	Art. 32(1)	"the controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk"	https://gdpr-info.eu/art-32-gdpr/	2026-04-27	standard	security-of-processing baseline; breach-prevention anchor

Cross-border transfer review	GDPR (Regulation 2016/679)	Art. 46(1)	"In the absence of a decision pursuant to Article 45(3), a controller or processor may transfer personal data to a third country or an international organisation only if the controller or processor has provided appropriate safeguards, and on condition that enforceable data subject rights and effective legal remedies for data subjects are available."	https://gdpr-info.eu/art-46-gdpr/	2026-04-27	standard	safeguards path when no adequacy decision
Cross-border transfer review	GDPR (Regulation 2016/679)	Art. 46(2)	"The appropriate safeguards referred to in paragraph 1 may be provided for, without requiring any specific authorisation from a supervisory authority, by: a legally binding and enforceable instrument between public authorities or bodies; binding corporate rules in accordance with Article 47; standard data protection clauses adopted by the Commission... standard data protection clauses adopted by a supervisory authority and approved by the Commission..."	https://gdpr-info.eu/art-46-gdpr/	2026-04-27	standard	SCC / BCR / approved-instrument enumeration
Cross-border transfer review	GDPR (Regulation 2016/679)	Art. 15(2)	"Where personal data are transferred to a third country or to an international organisation, the data subject shall have the right to be informed of the appropriate safeguards pursuant to Article 46 relating to the transfer."	https://gdpr-info.eu/art-15-gdpr/	2026-04-27	standard	transparency leg of cross-border transfer

Cross-border transfer review	GDPR (Regulation 2016/679)	Art. 28(3) opening	"Processing by a processor shall be governed by a contract or other legal act under Union or Member State law, that is binding on the processor with regard to the controller and that sets out the subject-matter and duration of the processing, the nature and purpose of the processing, the type of personal data and categories of data subjects and the obligations and rights of the controller."	https://gdpr-info.eu/art-28-gdpr/	2026-04-27	standard	processor-contract requirement; binds cross-border processor relationships

input.content:
# Applied walkthrough — Public Sector ATO Authorization Package

**Engagement archetype.** Federal civilian agency, DoD program office, or cleared defense contractor pursuing Authority to Operate (ATO) for a moderate- or high-impact information system. Could be a greenfield FedRAMP authorization for a SaaS offering, a CMMC Level 2 or Level 3 readiness for a CUI-handling DIB contractor, an eMASS package preparation for a DoD IT system, or an ICD 503 / JSIG cycle for an Intelligence Community or SAP system. Authorizing Official + System Owner + ISSM as co-buyers; CIO + CISO consulted on enterprise risk acceptance. Indicative SOW: 6–12 month assessment + remediation cycle; 4–6 parallel workstreams; $750K–$3M depending on baseline (Moderate vs. High vs. CMMC L3 vs. SAP) and scope of remediation engineering.

---

## §1 Industry overlay (regulatory regimes binding from day one)

| Regime | Scope | Authority surface |
|---|---|---|
| NIST RMF | every ATO workflow | NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2 — six-step lifecycle: Prepare, Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, Monitor |
| Control catalog | every system | NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 — security + privacy control catalog; FedRAMP Low/Moderate/High baselines derive from this |
| Assessment procedures | 3PAO + DIBCAC + IC assessors | NIST SP 800-53A Rev. 5 — assessment procedures matching the catalog |
| CUI protection | DIB contractors | NIST SP 800-171 Rev. 2 (110 controls, CMMC L2) + NIST SP 800-172 (enhanced, CMMC L3) |
| CMMC | DoD-prime + sub-tier contractors | 32 CFR § 170 — Final Rule; § 170.17 (L2 status types + 180-day POA&M), § 170.18 (DIBCAC L3 cycle) |
| FedRAMP | cloud service providers serving federal agencies | FedRAMP.gov Marketplace; Rev. 5 baselines aligned to NIST 800-53 Rev. 5; 3PAO assessment |
| DISA STIG | DoD-attached + cleared environments | XCCDF benchmarks per product/version on cyber.mil; CC SRG IL2/IL4/IL5/IL6 impact levels |
| ICD 503 | Intelligence Community systems | IC-CIO authority; references NIST + CNSSI 1253 categorization for NSS |
| JSIG | DoD Special Access Programs | JSIG = NIST 800-53 + CNSSI 1253 supplement under SAP AO; DoDM 5205.07 V1 umbrella |
| ConMon | post-ATO ongoing-authorization | NIST SP 800-137 + FedRAMP Continuous Monitoring Strategy Guide |

JD-corpus signal: federal + defense JDs cluster around ATO + STIG + CMMC + FedRAMP + clearance language; combined defense + public-sector overlay carries the highest regulatory density of any industry in the catalog.

---

## §2 Workflow profiles in scope (9 from the catalog's Public Sector / Defense cluster)

| # | Workflow | What it does | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ATO / RMF authorization | Authority to Operate via NIST SP 800-37 RMF six-step process | P14 + P11 binding |
| 2 | DISA STIG implementation | Security Technical Implementation Guide hardening + checklist per cyber.mil profiles | P14 + P05 binding |
| 3 | CMMC Level 2 readiness | Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification 110 NIST 800-171 controls; C3PAO assessment | P14 + P11 binding |
| 4 | CMMC Level 3 readiness | CMMC L3 with NIST SP 800-172 enhanced requirements; DIBCAC assessment; 3-year recertification | P14 + P11 binding |
| 5 | FedRAMP control implementation | Moderate / High baseline control implementation + 3PAO assessment | P14 + P05 binding |
| 6 | eMASS package preparation | DoD enterprise Mission Assurance Support Service authorization package | P14 + P34 binding |
| 7 | ICD 503 review | Intelligence Community Directive 503 risk-management framework | P14 + P11 binding |
| 8 | JSIG cycle | Joint SAP Implementation Guide cycle for special-access programs | P14 + P11 binding |
| 9 | Continuous Monitoring (ConMon) | FedRAMP / NIST SP 800-137 ongoing-authorization continuous monitoring | P14.4.a + P20 binding |

Each profile carries verbatim conformance citations + recommended OSS render repos in `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_workflows/clusters/05_public_sector_defense_*.tsv`.

---

## §3 Deliverables produced (12 catalog items across A → H phases)

Grouped by TOGAF ADM phase. Deliverable IDs reference the catalog's master deliverable register.

**Phase 0 — Preliminary**
- D-21 Architecture Repository (categorization artifacts + reference architecture)
- D-22 Tailored Architecture Framework (RMF + sector controls overlay)
- D-31 DORA Capability Assessment baseline (control-effectiveness baseline)

**Phase B–D — Definition**
- D-19 Threat Model and Data Protection Impact Assessment
- D-51 Identity and Access Management Plan (account-management AC controls)
- D-73 Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) (supply-chain SR controls)
- D-205 Continuous Compliance Telemetry (ConMon evidence stream)

**Phase E — Solutions**
- D-167 Algorithmic Impact Assessment (when ML/AI is in scope; AI-RMF overlay)

**Phase F — Migration**
- D-41 Performance Measurement Baseline (control-effectiveness measurement)

**Phase G — Implementation governance**
- D-8 Architecture Contract + Compliance Assessment (the SSP / SAR / POA&M trinity)

**Phase H — Change management**
- D-287 CMMC Plan (DoD-specific; enclave + system + assessment scoping)
- + 1 supporting deliverable (ID 99) attached at workflow grain

Total: **12 distinct deliverables**, each with template + conformance acceptance criteria + a buyer's-eye signoff checklist. The full ATO package itself maps to D-8 with NIST SP 800-37 Step 6 Authorize as the personhood-bound seal.

---

## §4 Conformance contracts (verbatim, with URL + accessed-at)

A representative slice of the 9-workflow conformance set. The full register lives at `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_workflows/clusters/05_public_sector_defense_conformance.tsv`.

**ATO / RMF authorization — NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2, RMF six-step process.**
> Step 1 Prepare: "Essential activities to prepare the organization to manage security and privacy risks"
> Step 2 Categorize: "Categorize the system and information processed, stored, and transmitted based on an impact analysis"
> Step 3 Select: "Select the set of NIST SP 800-53 controls to protect the system based on risk assessment(s)"
> Step 4 Implement: "Implement the controls and document how controls are deployed"
> Step 5 Assess: "Assess to determine if the controls are in place, operating as intended, and producing the desired results"
> Step 6 Authorize: "Senior official makes a risk-based decision to authorize the system (to operate)"
> https://csrc.nist.gov/projects/risk-management/about-rmf, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard.

**CMMC Level 2 — 32 CFR § 170.17(a)(1)(ii) Conditional L2 + POA&M timeline.**
> "a POA&M and the POA&M meets all CMMC Level 2 POA&M requirements" + "The OSC must remediate any NOT MET requirements...within 180 days of the CMMC Status Date"
> https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/32/170.17, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. Anchors the 180-day remediation window after Conditional L2.

**CMMC Level 3 — 32 CFR § 170.18 DIBCAC assessment + recertification.**
> "DCMA DIBCAC...will perform a Level 3 certification assessment in accordance with NIST SP 800-171A Jun2018" + "the Level 3 certification assessment must be performed every three years"
> https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/32/170.18, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. 3-year recert cycle is the subscription-revenue anchor.

**DISA STIG — CNSSI 4009-2015 / DoDI 8500.01 STIG definition.**
> "Implementation guide geared to a specific product and version. Contains all requirements that have been flagged as applicable for the product which have been selected on a DoD baseline."
> https://csrc.nist.gov/glossary/term/security_technical_implementation_guide, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard.

Pattern: every ATO deliverable's acceptance criteria close on NIST SP / 32 CFR / CNSSI verbatim language. The Authorizing Official validates against the regulation directly; the SSP + SAR + POA&M trinity is the artifact carrier.

---

## §5 Vendor stack (commercial layer, named)

| Layer | Vendor candidates | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| GRC + ATO platform | Telos Xacta, RSA Archer, ServiceNow GRC, RegScale, Ignyte | SSP + SAR + POA&M authoring + workflow |
| Continuous monitoring | Splunk Enterprise Security, Tenable.sc, Tenable Security Center, Elastic Security | FedRAMP-authorized SIEM + vuln-scan |
| Configuration + STIG hardening | Red Hat Satellite + OpenSCAP, Microsoft Endpoint Configuration Manager + DISA STIG, Puppet Enterprise Compliance | XCCDF profile remediation |
| Cloud platform (FedRAMP-authorized) | AWS GovCloud, Azure Government, Google Cloud Assured Workloads, Oracle Government Cloud | IL2 / IL4 / IL5 / IL6 alignment per CC SRG |
| Identity (federal) | Okta for Government, Microsoft Entra ID Government, SailPoint IdentityIQ | PIV / CAC / FIDO2 enforcement |
| Code analysis | Fortify on Demand (federal), Veracode (federal), Checkmarx, GitHub Advanced Security (FedRAMP Mod) | SA-11 developer security testing |

Per memory: the catalog stays vendor-agnostic at conformance grain; the vendor-stack table is buyer-facing context, not catalog content.

---

## §6 Render repos (OSS components for buy-vs-build sizing)

Drawn from `05_public_sector_defense_repos.tsv`. Stars + license + last-commit are the buy-vs-build signal.

| Workflow | Repo | Stars | License | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ATO / RMF, FedRAMP, eMASS, ICD 503, JSIG | usnistgov/OSCAL | 877 | NOASSERTION (NIST official) | canonical machine-readable format for SSP / SAR / SAP / POA&M |
| ATO / RMF, CMMC, FedRAMP, eMASS | oscal-compass/compliance-trestle | 250 | Apache-2.0 | IBM-led OSCAL toolchain; CI-driven RMF artifact generation |
| ATO / RMF, CMMC, FedRAMP | usnistgov/oscal-content | 430 | NOASSERTION | NIST 800-53 control catalog in OSCAL; FedRAMP example profiles |
| ATO / RMF | usnistgov/oscal-cli | 64 | NOASSERTION | OSCAL validation + conversion CLI; NIST official |
| DISA STIG, CMMC L2 | ComplianceAsCode/content | 2,711 | NOASSERTION | SCAP + Ansible/Bash remediation for STIG + 800-171 profiles |
| DISA STIG | OpenSCAP/openscap | 1,709 | LGPL-2.1 | NIST-certified SCAP 1.2 toolkit; consumes XCCDF STIG profiles |
| DISA STIG | dev-sec/ansible-collection-hardening | 5,326 | Apache-2.0 | hardening collection aligned to STIG / CIS baselines |
| DISA STIG | ansible-lockdown/RHEL8-STIG | 120 | MIT | RHEL 8 STIG remediation playbook |
| CMMC L2 + L3 | cisagov/cset | 1,819 | MIT | CISA's Cybersecurity Evaluation Tool; supports L1/L2/L3 self-assessment |
| FedRAMP | prowler-cloud/prowler | 13,688 | Apache-2.0 | FedRAMP-aligned compliance frameworks for AWS / Azure / GCP CSP environments |
| eMASS | mitre/heimdall2 | 249 | NOASSERTION | MITRE; consumes InSpec / SCAP results for eMASS upload |
| eMASS | mitre/inspec_tools | 97 | NOASSERTION | InSpec → CKL eMASS-format conversion (stale: last commit 2024-07; flagged) |
| ConMon | wazuh/wazuh | 15,429 | NOASSERTION | XDR + SIEM; ISCM-grade telemetry for FedRAMP ConMon |
| ConMon | aquasecurity/trivy | 34,737 | Apache-2.0 | monthly vuln-scan ConMon input |
| ConMon | CISOfy/lynis | 15,572 | GPL-3.0 | host-level continuous compliance scan; HIPAA / ISO27001 / PCI mappings |
| ConMon | aquasecurity/kube-bench | 8,033 | Apache-2.0 | K8s ConMon; CIS-aligned |

License flags: NOASSERTION on NIST + MITRE repos means US-government-work public-domain (effective Public-Domain in US, Apache-2.0-equivalent posture for international); copyleft on lynis (GPL-3.0) + openscap (LGPL-2.1) fires the buyer's IP review for derivative-distribution scenarios; permissive on Apache-2.0 / MIT components is buy-vs-extend without IP friction.

---

## §7 Cert paths the staffing stack draws from

| Cert | Authority | Workflow scope |
|---|---|---|
| CISSP | (ISC)² | enterprise security architect; baseline for ISSM / ISSO roles |
| CAP / CGRC | (ISC)² | RMF-specific certification; SSP authoring authority |
| CMMC Certified Professional (CCP) | Cyber-AB | CMMC L2 readiness; pre-assessment role |
| CMMC Certified Assessor (CCA) | Cyber-AB | CMMC L2 C3PAO assessment delivery |
| FedRAMP 3PAO Lead Assessor | FedRAMP PMO | FedRAMP package assessment delivery |
| Security+ CE | CompTIA | DoD 8570 / 8140 baseline for IAT II positions |
| CISM | ISACA | governance + risk management at the program level |
| AWS Certified Security – Specialty | AWS | cloud-control implementation in GovCloud + commercial regions |

---

## §8 Authority closes (engagement-level, tier-ordered)

**Principle.** Defense in depth (Saltzer + Schroeder, 1975 — "Design Principles for the Protection of Information in Computer Systems"; CACM): no single control is the gate; layered controls compensate for individual failures. Every ATO package implements this principle through the 800-53 control catalog's overlapping families. https://www.acsac.org/secshelf/papers/protection_information.pdf, accessed 2026-04-28.

**Framework.** NIST RMF (SP 800-37 Rev. 2): the six-step lifecycle is the framework that organizes the entire engagement. Maps directly to the catalog's P14 binding. https://csrc.nist.gov/projects/risk-management/about-rmf, accessed 2026-04-27.

**Standard.** NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 control catalog + NIST SP 800-53A Rev. 5 assessment procedures + 32 CFR § 170 (CMMC Final Rule) all verbatim in §4 above. https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/53/r5/upd1/final, accessed 2026-04-27; https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/32/170, accessed 2026-04-27.

---

## §9 What this walkthrough demonstrates about the catalog

1. **The catalog already knows this engagement.** 9 workflow profiles + 12 deliverables + 6 verbatim conformance citations + 16 OSS repos + 8 cert paths fall out of the existing substrate.
2. **OSCAL is the artifact spine.** Every public-sector workflow's render repos converge on the OSCAL ecosystem (NIST + IBM + MITRE) — meaning the catalog's deliverable IDs map cleanly to OSCAL JSON/XML for buyer-side ingestion into eMASS / Xacta / ServiceNow GRC.
3. **The 180-day POA&M window is the recurring-revenue anchor.** CMMC L2 Conditional status with a POA&M creates a 180-day remediation engagement; Final L2 + 3-year L3 recert creates the subscription-cadence revenue model the catalog quietly enables.
4. **NIST + 32 CFR + CNSSI are the verbatim authority spine.** Unlike commercial engagements where multiple frameworks compete, public-sector buyers validate against a tightly bounded canonical set; the verbatim-citation discipline is non-optional rather than a differentiator.

**Pattern-extension.** Same eight-section template ran on Healthcare RCM at `applied_walkthrough_healthcare_rcm.md`; runs next on DevOps/SRE Reliability + GDPR/CCPA Privacy via `clusters/08_devops_sre_*.tsv` + `clusters/07_data_privacy_*.tsv`.

| DISA STIG | dev-sec/ansible-collection-hardening | 5,326 | Apache-2.0 | hardening collection aligned to STIG / CIS baselines |
| DISA STIG | ansible-lockdown/RHEL8-STIG | 120 | MIT | RHEL 8 STIG remediation playbook |
| CMMC L2 + L3 | cisagov/cset | 1,819 | MIT | CISA's Cybersecurity Evaluation Tool; supports L1/L2/L3 self-assessment |
| FedRAMP | prowler-cloud/prowler | 13,688 | Apache-2.0 | FedRAMP-aligned compliance frameworks for AWS / Azure / GCP CSP environments |
| eMASS | mitre/heimdall2 | 249 | NOASSERTION | MITRE; consumes InSpec / SCAP results for eMASS upload |
| eMASS | mitre/inspec_tools | 97 | NOASSERTION | InSpec → CKL eMASS-format conversion (stale: last commit 2024-07; flagged) |
| ConMon | wazuh/wazuh | 15,429 | NOASSERTION | XDR + SIEM; ISCM-grade telemetry for FedRAMP ConMon |
| ConMon | aquasecurity/trivy | 34,737 | Apache-2.0 | monthly vuln-scan ConMon input |
| ConMon | CISOfy/lynis | 15,572 | GPL-3.0 | host-level continuous compliance scan; HIPAA / ISO27001 / PCI mappings |
| ConMon | aquasecurity/kube-bench | 8,033 | Apache-2.0 | K8s ConMon; CIS-aligned |

License flags: NOASSERTION on NIST + MITRE repos means US-government-work public-domain (effective Public-Domain in US, Apache-2.0-equivalent posture for international); copyleft on lynis (GPL-3.0) + openscap (LGPL-2.1) fires the buyer's IP review for derivative-distribution scenarios; permissive on Apache-2.0 / MIT components is buy-vs-extend without IP friction.

---

## §7 Cert paths the staffing stack draws from

| Cert | Authority | Workflow scope |
|---|---|---|
| CISSP | (ISC)² | enterprise security architect; baseline for ISSM / ISSO roles |
| CAP / CGRC | (ISC)² | RMF-specific certification; SSP authoring authority |
| CMMC Certified Professional (CCP) | Cyber-AB | CMMC L2 readiness; pre-assessment role |
| CMMC Certified Assessor (CCA) | Cyber-AB | CMMC L2 C3PAO assessment delivery |
| FedRAMP 3PAO Lead Assessor | FedRAMP PMO | FedRAMP package assessment delivery |
| Security+ CE | CompTIA | DoD 8570 / 8140 baseline for IAT II positions |
| CISM | ISACA | governance + risk management at the program level |
| AWS Certified Security – Specialty | AWS | cloud-control implementation in GovCloud + commercial regions |

---

## §8 Authority closes (engagement-level, tier-ordered)

**Principle.** Defense in depth (Saltzer + Schroeder, 1975 — "Design Principles for the Protection of Information in Computer Systems"; CACM): no single control is the gate; layered controls compensate for individual failures. Every ATO package implements this principle through the 800-53 control catalog's overlapping families. https://www.acsac.org/secshelf/papers/protection_information.pdf, accessed 2026-04-28.

**Framework.** NIST RMF (SP 800-37 Rev. 2): the six-step lifecycle is the framework that organizes the entire engagement. Maps directly to the catalog's P14 binding. https://csrc.nist.gov/projects/risk-management/about-rmf, accessed 2026-04-27.

**Standard.** NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 control catalog + NIST SP 800-53A Rev. 5 assessment procedures + 32 CFR § 170 (CMMC Final Rule) all verbatim in §4 above. https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/53/r5/upd1/final, accessed 2026-04-27; https://www.law.cornell.edu/cfr/text/32/170, accessed 2026-04-27.

---

## §9 What this walkthrough demonstrates about the catalog

1. **The catalog already knows this engagement.** 9 workflow profiles + 12 deliverables + 6 verbatim conformance citations + 16 OSS repos + 8 cert paths fall out of the existing substrate.
2. **OSCAL is the artifact spine.** Every public-sector workflow's render repos converge on the OSCAL ecosystem (NIST + IBM + MITRE) — meaning the catalog's deliverable IDs map cleanly to OSCAL JSON/XML for buyer-side ingestion into eMASS / Xacta / ServiceNow GRC.
3. **The 180-day POA&M window is the recurring-revenue anchor.** CMMC L2 Conditional status with a POA&M creates a 180-day remediation engagement; Final L2 + 3-year L3 recert creates the subscription-cadence revenue model the catalog quietly enables.
4. **NIST + 32 CFR + CNSSI are the verbatim authority spine.** Unlike commercial engagements where multiple frameworks compete, public-sector buyers validate against a tightly bounded canonical set; the verbatim-citation discipline is non-optional rather than a differentiator.

**Pattern-extension.** Same eight-section template ran on Healthcare RCM at `applied_walkthrough_healthcare_rcm.md`; runs next on DevOps/SRE Reliability + GDPR/CCPA Privacy via `clusters/08_devops_sre_*.tsv` + `clusters/07_data_privacy_*.tsv`.

# Applied walkthrough — DevOps/SRE Reliability Engineering Engagement

**Engagement archetype.** Mid-to-large software organization (50–500 engineers across 5–30 services) standing up or modernizing the reliability practice: SLOs + error budgets + on-call + incident response + post-incident review + change management + release pipeline + chaos engineering + DR. Could be a SaaS company maturing from "ops by heroics" to a measured SRE practice, an enterprise consolidating fragmented incident-response across business units, or a regulated-industry IT org installing ITIL 4 Change Enablement on top of an existing CI/CD stack. VP Engineering + Director of Platform + CTO as co-buyers; CISO consulted on incident-response leg. Indicative SOW: 12-week assessment + 16-week installation + 12-week measurement; 5–7 parallel workstreams; $500K–$1.5M depending on scope of platform-team enablement + tooling integration.

---

## §1 Industry overlay (frameworks + standards binding from day one)

| Framework / Standard | Scope | Authority surface |
|---|---|---|
| NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 | incident response lifecycle | four-phase: Preparation, Detection + Analysis, Containment + Eradication + Recovery, Post-Incident Activity (Rev. 2 withdrawn 2025-04-03; Rev. 3 supersedes; Rev. 2 phrasing remains canonical in industry use) |
| ITIL 4 Change Enablement (P27) | change management | RFC + CAB + change types (Standard / Normal / Emergency); change authority tiering |
| Google SRE Book + Workbook | SLO / error-budget / on-call / postmortem | Ch 3 Embracing Risk, Ch 5 Alerting on SLOs (Workbook), Ch 11 Being On-Call, Ch 14 Managing Incidents, Ch 15 Postmortem Culture, Ch 9 Incident Response (Workbook) |
| DORA Four Keys | release-cycle measurement | deployment frequency + change lead time + change failure rate + failed deployment recovery time |
| Martin Fowler bliki | release patterns | canary release + blue-green deployment + feature toggles canonical definitions |
| FIRST.org CVSS v3.1 + v4.0 | vulnerability triage | base + temporal + environmental + supplemental (v4.0) metrics; severity rating scale 0.0–10.0 |
| NIST SP 800-40 Rev. 4 | enterprise patch management | identify + prioritize + acquire + install + verify lifecycle |
| Principles of Chaos Engineering | chaos experiments | steady state + hypothesis + real-world variables + minimize blast radius |
| NIST CSRC glossary | DR + tabletop terminology | disaster recovery plan + tabletop exercise per CNSSI 4009-2015 / SP 800-34 / SP 800-84 |

JD-corpus signal: SRE + DevOps + Platform Engineering JDs cluster around SLOs + incident response + GitOps + Kubernetes + Terraform + observability; ITIL 4 mentioned in 38 of 22,960 JDs primarily in regulated-industry-IT contexts.

---

## §2 Workflow profiles in scope (9 from the catalog's DevOps / SRE cluster)

| # | Workflow | What it does | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Incident response (NIST SP 800-61) | preparation; detection; containment; eradication; recovery; lessons-learned | P14.3 + P22 binding |
| 2 | Change management (ITIL P27) | RFC; CAB review; implementation; review per ITIL 4 Change Enablement | P27 binding (direct) |
| 3 | Release cycle | feature; staging; canary; production with feature-flag rollout | P31 + P29 binding |
| 4 | On-call rotation | primary / secondary / SME tiers with paging policy + 50% engineering / 25% on-call ceiling | P14 + P07 binding |
| 5 | Post-incident review (blameless) | Google SRE blameless postmortem + action items | P14.3.d + P17 binding |
| 6 | Error-budget burn-rate response | SLO burn-rate alerting + freeze policy | P19 + P20 binding |
| 7 | Vulnerability triage | CVE detection; severity (CVSS); patch / mitigate / accept | P14.4 + P22 binding |
| 8 | Chaos engineering experiment | Principles of Chaos Engineering: hypothesis; experiment; measure | P30 + P14 binding |
| 9 | Disaster recovery test | tabletop + functional + full-cutover DR exercises | P12 + P30 binding |

Each profile carries verbatim conformance citations + recommended OSS render repos in `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_workflows/clusters/08_devops_sre_*.tsv`.

---

## §3 Deliverables produced (26 catalog items across A → H phases)

Grouped by TOGAF ADM phase. Deliverable IDs reference the catalog's master deliverable register.

**Phase A — Vision**
- D-26 Resource Management Plan and RACI Matrix (on-call + incident-commander + ops-lead roles)
- D-43 Integrated Change Control Process (change-authority tier policy)
- D-92 Branching and Release Strategy (trunk-based + feature-flag + canary)
- D-126 Sprint / Release Calendar (release-train cadence)

**Phase B–D — Definition**
- D-18 SLI / SLO / SLA Register (error-budget formation)
- D-19 Threat Model and DPIA (vulnerability + threat surface)
- D-73 Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) (supply-chain attack surface)
- D-112 Vulnerability Disclosure Policy (CVD + CNA scope)
- D-115 Quality Metrics Library (DORA Four Keys + SLO compliance)
- D-117 Coordinated Disclosure / CVE Handling Procedure
- D-123 Incident Severity Classification Schema (SEV1–SEV4 ladder)

**Phase E — Solutions**
- D-4 Architecture Roadmap with Transition Architectures
- D-33 Test Strategy (chaos + DR test plans)
- D-84 Chaos Engineering Plan

**Phase F — Migration**
- D-20 Operating Model and Runbook (per-service runbook library)
- D-34 Disaster Recovery and Business Continuity Plan
- D-128 Disaster Recovery and Tabletop Exercise Plan
- D-145 Service Owner Assignment (SLO ownership accountability)
- D-158 Incident Communication Plan (3-Cs: coordinate + communicate + control)
- D-159 Production Readiness Review (PRR) Checklist
- D-162 Backout / Rollback Plan Template

**Phase G — Implementation governance**
- D-8 Architecture Contract + Compliance Assessment

**Phase H — Change management**
- D-35 Architecture Change Management Plan
- D-36 Issue Log and Lessons Learned Register (postmortem-action-tracking)
- + 2 supporting deliverables (IDs 24, 54) attached at workflow grain

Total: **26 distinct deliverables** — the largest deliverable count among the four archetypes, reflecting that DevOps/SRE engagements touch every phase of TOGAF ADM rather than concentrating in a regulated-control phase.

---

## §4 Conformance contracts (verbatim, with URL + accessed-at)

A representative slice of the 9-workflow conformance set. The full register lives at `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_workflows/clusters/08_devops_sre_conformance.tsv`.

**Incident response — NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2, four-phase lifecycle.**
> "four-phase incident response life cycle: Preparation; Detection and Analysis; Containment, Eradication, and Recovery; Post-Incident Activity"
> https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/61/r2/final, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard (Rev. 2 withdrawn 2025-04-03 / superseded by Rev. 3 but phrasing remains canonical in industry use).

**Incident response — Google SRE Workbook Ch 9, three-Cs principle.**
> "Coordinate response effort. Communicate between incident responders, within the organization, and to the outside world. Maintain control over the incident response."
> https://sre.google/workbook/incident-response/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: framework.

**Error-budget burn-rate — Google SRE Workbook Ch 5, multi-window thresholds.**
> "Page / 1 hour / 5 minutes / burn rate 14.4 / 2% budget consumed; Page / 6 hours / 30 minutes / burn rate 6 / 5%; Ticket / 3 days / 6 hours / burn rate 1 / 10%"
> https://sre.google/workbook/alerting-on-slos/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: framework. The recommended thresholds for a 99.9% SLO; alerts wire directly to these numbers in Prometheus / sloth.

**Change management — ITIL 4 change types.**
> "Standard Changes: Pre-authorized, low-risk Changes that follow a well-known procedure. Emergency Changes: Changes that must be implemented immediately, for example to resolve a Major Incident. Normal Changes: All other Changes"
> https://wiki.en.it-processmaps.com/index.php/Change_Management, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: framework (AXELOS practice-guide body paywalled; IT Process Maps wiki tracks ITIL terminology).

**Release cycle — DORA Four Keys.**
> Deployment frequency: "The number of deployments over a given period or the time between deployments."
> Change lead time: "The amount of time it takes for a change to go from committed to version control to deployed in production."
> Change failure rate: "The ratio of deployments that require immediate intervention following a deployment."
> Failed deployment recovery time: "The time it takes to recover from a deployment that fails and requires immediate intervention."
> https://dora.dev/guides/dora-metrics-four-keys/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: framework.

**Chaos engineering — principlesofchaos.org.**
> "Chaos Engineering is the discipline of experimenting on a system in order to build confidence in the system's capability to withstand turbulent conditions in production."
> https://principlesofchaos.org/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: principle.

Pattern: every reliability deliverable's acceptance criteria close on at least one verbatim citation from NIST + Google SRE + DORA + Martin Fowler + principlesofchaos. The buyer's platform team validates the deliverable against the canonical text directly.

---

## §5 Vendor stack (commercial layer, named)

| Layer | Vendor candidates | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Incident response + on-call | PagerDuty, Opsgenie (Atlassian), incident.io, FireHydrant, Rootly | paging + war-room + status-page + postmortem authoring |
| Observability + SLO tracking | Datadog, New Relic, Honeycomb, Grafana Cloud, Splunk Observability, Lightstep (ServiceNow) | metrics + traces + logs + SLO dashboards |
| Change management (ITIL) | ServiceNow ITSM, BMC Helix ITSM, Jira Service Management | RFC + CAB + change record |
| Release pipeline | GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI, Jenkins, Spinnaker, Harness, Octopus Deploy | CI + CD orchestration |
| GitOps + progressive delivery | Argo CD, Argo Rollouts, Flux CD, Codefresh | declarative deploy + canary/blue-green |
| Vulnerability + supply-chain | Snyk, Mend, Sonatype Lifecycle, JFrog Xray, GitHub Advanced Security | SCA + container scanning + license compliance |
| Chaos engineering | Gremlin, Steadybit, Chaos Monkey (Netflix), AWS Fault Injection Simulator | hypothesis + experiment + blast-radius control |
| DR testing | CloudEndure (AWS DRS), Zerto, Veeam, Druva | full-cutover failover orchestration |

Per memory: the catalog stays vendor-agnostic at conformance grain; the vendor-stack table is buyer-facing context, not catalog content.

---

## §6 Render repos (OSS components for buy-vs-build sizing)

Drawn from `08_devops_sre_repos.tsv`. Stars + license + last-commit are the buy-vs-build signal.

| Workflow | Repo | Stars | License | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Incident response | grafana/oncall | 3,900 | AGPL-3.0 | Slack-integrated paging (archived 2026-03-24 / read-only; AGPL fires source-disclosure on network use) |
| Incident response | incidentbot/incidentbot | 160 | MIT | Slack ChatOps bot with Jira / PagerDuty / Statuspage integrations |
| Incident response, on-call | linkedin/oncall | 1,200 | BSD-2-Clause | calendar tool for on-call shift scheduling |
| Change management | argoproj/argo-cd | 22,700 | Apache-2.0 | declarative GitOps; PR-as-RFC + audit trail for every change |
| Change management | fluxcd/flux2 | 8,100 | Apache-2.0 | CNCF-graduated GitOps; same change-as-PR workflow |
| Release cycle | argoproj/argo-rollouts | 3,500 | Apache-2.0 | canonical canary / blue-green controller for K8s; metric-driven promotion |
| Release cycle | spinnaker/spinnaker | 9,700 | Apache-2.0 | multi-cloud release pipelines with canary analysis |
| Post-incident review | dastergon/postmortem-templates | 1,400 | CC0-1.0 | template library derived from SRE Book + The Practice of Cloud System Administration |
| Error-budget | slok/sloth | 2,500 | Apache-2.0 | canonical Prometheus SLO generator; implements Workbook Ch 5 multi-window thresholds |
| Error-budget | OpenSLO/OpenSLO | 1,500 | Apache-2.0 | vendor-agnostic SLO YAML specification |
| Vulnerability triage | aquasecurity/trivy | 34,700 | Apache-2.0 | canonical CVE scanner for cloud-native; v0.70.0 Apr 2026 |
| Vulnerability triage | anchore/grype | 12,100 | Apache-2.0 | container + filesystem vuln scanner; SBOM-aware via syft |
| Vulnerability triage | dependabot/dependabot-core | 5,600 | MIT | canonical dep-update bot core; multi-language |
| Vulnerability triage | falcosecurity/falco | 8,900 | Apache-2.0 | CNCF-graduated runtime threat detection (eBPF) |
| Chaos engineering, DR test | chaos-mesh/chaos-mesh | 7,600 | Apache-2.0 | K8s-native chaos platform; web UI + CRDs |
| Chaos engineering, DR test | litmuschaos/litmus | 5,300 | Apache-2.0 | CNCF-incubating chaos platform |

License flags: AGPL-3.0 on grafana/oncall fires the buyer's IP review for network-use scenarios (SaaS deployment triggers source-disclosure obligations); CC0-1.0 on postmortem-templates is public-domain dedication (no IP friction); Apache-2.0 + MIT + BSD on the rest is buy-vs-extend without IP friction. Canonical OSS coverage is strong across every workflow except DR test (the canonical orchestrators are still commercial: CloudEndure, Zerto, Veeam).

---

## §7 Cert paths the staffing stack draws from

| Cert | Authority | Workflow scope |
|---|---|---|
| ITIL 4 Foundation | PeopleCert | change management baseline |
| ITIL 4 Specialist: Create, Deliver and Support | PeopleCert | full SVC operational practices |
| Google Cloud Professional Cloud DevOps Engineer | Google | SRE practice + SLO + error budgeting |
| AWS Certified DevOps Engineer – Professional | AWS | release pipeline + IaC + monitoring |
| Azure DevOps Engineer Expert | Microsoft | release pipeline + observability |
| CKA + CKS (Certified Kubernetes Administrator + Specialist) | CNCF | K8s-native release + chaos + vulnerability scope |
| Datadog Certified Professional | Datadog | observability + SLO ownership |
| PagerDuty Foundations + Practitioner | PagerDuty | incident response orchestration |

---

## §8 Authority closes (engagement-level, tier-ordered)

**Principle.** Embracing Risk (Google SRE Book Ch 3): perfect availability is the wrong target; the right target is the SLO that allows release velocity within an acceptable error budget. The error budget is the engineering decision the rest of the practice composes from. https://sre.google/sre-book/embracing-risk/, accessed 2026-04-27.

**Framework.** ITIL 4 Change Enablement (P27) for change management + Google SRE Book / Workbook for SLO + on-call + postmortem + DORA Four Keys for release-cycle measurement. The catalog binds change management at P27 (direct) and the SRE practices at P14 + P19 + P20.

**Standard.** NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 four-phase incident lifecycle + FIRST.org CVSS v3.1 / v4.0 severity rating + NIST SP 800-40 Rev. 4 patch management lifecycle, all verbatim in §4 above. https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/61/r2/final, https://www.first.org/cvss/v3.1/specification-document, https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/40/r4/final, all accessed 2026-04-27.

---

## §9 What this walkthrough demonstrates about the catalog

1. **The catalog already knows this engagement.** 9 workflow profiles + 26 deliverables + 6 verbatim conformance citations + 16 OSS repos + 8 cert paths fall out of the existing substrate.
2. **DevOps/SRE has the largest deliverable count of the four archetypes.** 26 deliverables vs. 21 (Healthcare RCM), 12 (Public Sector ATO), 18 (Privacy) — meaning reliability engagements touch every TOGAF ADM phase rather than concentrating in a regulated-control phase. The catalog absorbs this without proliferating new categories.
3. **OSS coverage is near-complete for cloud-native workflows but thin for DR testing.** Argo + Flux + sloth + trivy + grype + chaos-mesh + litmus together cover ~80% of the engagement's tooling needs in OSS; commercial spend concentrates on observability platforms + ITSM + DR-cutover orchestration.
4. **The error budget is the keystone abstraction.** SLI / SLO / SLA Register (D-18) drives error-budget burn-rate response (Workflow #6), which gates the release cycle (Workflow #3), which generates the change records (Workflow #2), which feeds postmortems (Workflow #5) when failed. The catalog's deliverable + workflow graph naturally walks the buyer through this composition.

**Pattern-extension.** Same eight-section template ran on Healthcare RCM at `applied_walkthrough_healthcare_rcm.md` + Public Sector ATO at `applied_walkthrough_public_sector_ato.md`; runs next on GDPR/CCPA Privacy via `clusters/07_data_privacy_*.tsv`.

**Incident response — NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2, four-phase lifecycle.**
> "four-phase incident response life cycle: Preparation; Detection and Analysis; Containment, Eradication, and Recovery; Post-Incident Activity"
> https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/61/r2/final, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard (Rev. 2 withdrawn 2025-04-03 / superseded by Rev. 3 but phrasing remains canonical in industry use).

**Incident response — Google SRE Workbook Ch 9, three-Cs principle.**
> "Coordinate response effort. Communicate between incident responders, within the organization, and to the outside world. Maintain control over the incident response."
> https://sre.google/workbook/incident-response/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: framework.

**Error-budget burn-rate — Google SRE Workbook Ch 5, multi-window thresholds.**
> "Page / 1 hour / 5 minutes / burn rate 14.4 / 2% budget consumed; Page / 6 hours / 30 minutes / burn rate 6 / 5%; Ticket / 3 days / 6 hours / burn rate 1 / 10%"
> https://sre.google/workbook/alerting-on-slos/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: framework. The recommended thresholds for a 99.9% SLO; alerts wire directly to these numbers in Prometheus / sloth.

**Change management — ITIL 4 change types.**
> "Standard Changes: Pre-authorized, low-risk Changes that follow a well-known procedure. Emergency Changes: Changes that must be implemented immediately, for example to resolve a Major Incident. Normal Changes: All other Changes"
> https://wiki.en.it-processmaps.com/index.php/Change_Management, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: framework (AXELOS practice-guide body paywalled; IT Process Maps wiki tracks ITIL terminology).

**Release cycle — DORA Four Keys.**
> Deployment frequency: "The number of deployments over a given period or the time between deployments."
> Change lead time: "The amount of time it takes for a change to go from committed to version control to deployed in production."
> Change failure rate: "The ratio of deployments that require immediate intervention following a deployment."
> Failed deployment recovery time: "The time it takes to recover from a deployment that fails and requires immediate intervention."
> https://dora.dev/guides/dora-metrics-four-keys/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: framework.

**Chaos engineering — principlesofchaos.org.**
> "Chaos Engineering is the discipline of experimenting on a system in order to build confidence in the system's capability to withstand turbulent conditions in production."
> https://principlesofchaos.org/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: principle.

Pattern: every reliability deliverable's acceptance criteria close on at least one verbatim citation from NIST + Google SRE + DORA + Martin Fowler + principlesofchaos. The buyer's platform team validates the deliverable against the canonical text directly.

**Framework.** ITIL 4 Change Enablement (P27) for change management + Google SRE Book / Workbook for SLO + on-call + postmortem + DORA Four Keys for release-cycle measurement. The catalog binds change management at P27 (direct) and the SRE practices at P14 + P19 + P20.
