# Applied walkthrough — GDPR/CCPA Data Privacy Program

**Engagement archetype.** Mid-to-large organization (1,000–25,000 employees) installing or modernizing a privacy program: DSAR pipeline + consent management + data lineage + RoPA + DPIA workflow + retention enforcement + breach response + cross-border transfer governance. Could be a US-headquartered SaaS company building EU + UK + California compliance at once after a market-expansion trigger, a multinational re-baselining after the EU-US Data Privacy Framework + state-privacy-law expansion (Virginia + Colorado + Connecticut + Utah + Texas + Florida + Oregon + Iowa + Indiana + Tennessee + Montana), or a regulated-industry organization (healthcare, finance, ad-tech) layering privacy controls on top of an existing security program. DPO + Chief Privacy Officer + General Counsel as co-buyers; CISO + CDO consulted on data-discovery + breach-response legs. Indicative SOW: 12-week assessment + 24-week installation + ongoing managed-DPO retainer; 4–6 parallel workstreams; $400K–$1.5M for the build phase + $200K–$600K annual recurring for the managed leg.

---

## §1 Industry overlay (regulatory regimes binding from day one)

| Regime | Scope | Authority surface |
|---|---|---|
| GDPR (EU) | every workflow touching EU/EEA personal data | Articles 5 (principles) + 7 (consent) + 12–22 (data subject rights) + 25 (privacy by design) + 28 (processor) + 30 (RoPA) + 32–34 (security + breach) + 35 (DPIA) + 44–49 (transfers) |
| UK GDPR + Data Protection Act 2018 | UK personal data | UK GDPR mirrors EU GDPR with UK-specific schedules; ICO is the supervisory authority |
| CCPA / CPRA (California) | California consumer personal information | Cal. Civ. Code §1798.100 series; §1798.105 (deletion), §1798.120 (opt-out of sale/share) |
| US state privacy laws | residents of VA, CO, CT, UT, TX, FL, OR, IA, IN, TN, MT, OH, NJ, DE, NH, NE, KY, RI, MN, MD | varying scope, opt-out + sensitive-data + DPIA-equivalent + universal opt-out; each enacted 2023–2026 |
| HIPAA + HITECH | healthcare-overlapping use cases | breach notification overlaps with GDPR Art. 33 timing |
| EU SCC (Implementing Decision 2021/914) | cross-border transfers EU → third countries | modernized SCC modules; controller-to-controller, controller-to-processor, processor-to-processor |
| NIST Privacy Framework v1.0 | program structure | Identify-P + Govern-P + Control-P + Communicate-P + Protect-P functions |
| ISO/IEC 27701:2019 | PIMS extension to ISO 27001 | privacy information management system; certifiable atop ISO 27001 |
| DAMA-DMBOK 2 | data management knowledge | Ch 5 Data Modeling, Ch 10 Reference + Master Data, Ch 11 Data Warehousing + Lineage, Ch 13 Data Quality |

JD-corpus signal: privacy roles cluster around DSAR + GDPR + CCPA + DPO + privacy-engineering + data-governance language; cross-industry overlay (every industry ≥ certain size triggers privacy program).

---

## §2 Workflow profiles in scope (10 from the catalog's Data Privacy cluster)

| # | Workflow | What it does | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GDPR DSAR processing | Data Subject Access Request per GDPR Articles 12–15 + CCPA §1798.105 | P34 + P14 binding |
| 2 | Consent renewal cycle | GDPR consent re-collection + withdrawal handling + symmetric-withdrawal compliance | P34 + P32 binding |
| 3 | Data lineage discovery | automated + manual data-lineage discovery via metadata harvest | P01 + P34 binding |
| 4 | MDM cleanup cycle | Master Data Management deduplication + survivorship | P01 + P34 binding |
| 5 | Data quality issue resolution | DQ rule violation; investigation; fix; root cause | P22 + P01 binding |
| 6 | Schema evolution review | backward-compatible schema change per data contract | P27 + P29 binding |
| 7 | Data retention enforcement | retention-period enforcement + deletion per GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) | P34 + P14 binding |
| 8 | Privacy Impact Assessment (DPIA) | GDPR Article 35 DPIA for high-risk processing | P11 + P14 binding |
| 9 | Data breach notification | GDPR Article 33 72-hour notification to supervisory authority | P14.3 + P32 binding |
| 10 | Cross-border transfer review | GDPR Chapter V transfer mechanism (SCCs / BCRs / adequacy) | P14 + P08 binding |

Each profile carries verbatim conformance citations + recommended OSS render repos in `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_workflows/clusters/07_data_privacy_*.tsv`.

---

## §3 Deliverables produced (18 catalog items across A → H phases)

Grouped by TOGAF ADM phase. Deliverable IDs reference the catalog's master deliverable register.

**Phase 0 — Preliminary**
- D-40 Privacy Notice and Records Retention Schedule
- D-50 Data Governance Framework

**Phase B–D — Definition**
- D-19 Threat Model and Data Protection Impact Assessment
- D-64 Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) (GDPR Art. 30)
- D-98 Data Subject Access Request (DSAR) Procedure (GDPR Art. 12–22 + CCPA)
- D-108 Data Retention and Disposal Procedures (GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) + Art. 17)
- D-115 Quality Metrics Library (DQ rules + DPIA risk metrics)
- D-122 API Deprecation Policy (schema evolution governance)
- D-124 Cookie Policy and Consent Management (GDPR Art. 7 + ePrivacy + CPRA opt-out)
- D-131 Records Hold (Litigation Hold) Policy (overrides retention deletion under legal hold)
- D-151 Cross-Border Data Transfer Mechanism (SCC + BCR + adequacy decision)
- D-155 Data Lineage Documentation
- D-213 Data Catalog (PII + sensitive data classification)
- D-215 MDM Strategy (survivorship under accuracy + minimization principles)

**Phase F — Migration**
- D-72 Breach Notification Plan (GDPR Art. 33 72-hour timer + state-AG matrix)
- D-158 Incident Communication Plan (DPO + supervisory-authority + data-subject comms)

**Phase G — Implementation governance**
- + 2 supporting deliverables (IDs 63, 121) attached at workflow grain

Total: **18 distinct deliverables**, each with template + conformance acceptance criteria + a buyer's-eye signoff checklist.

---

## §4 Conformance contracts (verbatim, with URL + accessed-at)

A representative slice of the 10-workflow conformance set. The full register lives at `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_workflows/clusters/07_data_privacy_conformance.tsv`.

**DSAR — GDPR Art. 12(3) one-month timer.**
> "The controller shall provide information on action taken on a request under Articles 15 to 22 to the data subject without undue delay and in any event within one month of receipt of the request."
> https://gdpr-info.eu/art-12-gdpr/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. Anchors the DSAR pipeline's SLA design.

**DSAR — CCPA §1798.105(c)(1) downstream propagation.**
> "A business that receives a verifiable consumer request from a consumer to delete the consumer's personal information... shall delete the consumer's personal information from its records, notify any service providers or contractors to delete... and notify all third parties to whom the business has sold or shared the personal information to delete"
> https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=CIV&sectionNum=1798.105., accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. The downstream-propagation obligation is what makes lineage + processor-tracking non-optional.

**Consent — GDPR Art. 7(3) symmetric withdrawal.**
> "The data subject shall have the right to withdraw his or her consent at any time. The withdrawal of consent shall not affect the lawfulness of processing based on consent before its withdrawal. Prior to giving consent, the data subject shall be informed thereof. It shall be as easy to withdraw as to give consent."
> https://gdpr-info.eu/art-7-gdpr/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. The "as easy to withdraw as to give" constraint dictates UX symmetry in the consent-management module.

**Breach notification — GDPR Art. 33(1) 72-hour timer.**
> "The controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority"
> https://gdpr-info.eu/art-33-gdpr/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. The 72-hour timer is the program's most-cited regulatory clock.

**DPIA — GDPR Art. 35(7) minimum content.**
> "The assessment shall contain at least: a systematic description of the envisaged processing operations and the purposes... an assessment of the necessity and proportionality... an assessment of the risks to the rights and freedoms of data subjects... and the measures envisaged to address the risks, including safeguards, security measures and mechanisms"
> https://gdpr-info.eu/art-35-gdpr/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. Defines the DPIA template's required sections.

**Cross-border transfer — GDPR Art. 46(2) safeguards enumeration.**
> "The appropriate safeguards referred to in paragraph 1 may be provided for, without requiring any specific authorisation from a supervisory authority, by: a legally binding and enforceable instrument between public authorities or bodies; binding corporate rules in accordance with Article 47; standard data protection clauses adopted by the Commission..."
> https://gdpr-info.eu/art-46-gdpr/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. The decision tree for cross-border transfer review derives from this enumeration.

**RoPA — GDPR Art. 30(1).**
> "Each controller and, where applicable, the controller's representative, shall maintain a record of processing activities under its responsibility."
> https://gdpr-info.eu/art-30-gdpr/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. The RoPA is the lineage-anchor obligation that data-discovery feeds.

Pattern: every privacy deliverable's acceptance criteria close on at least one verbatim citation from GDPR + CCPA + the NIST Privacy Framework + ISO/IEC 27701 + DAMA-DMBOK. The DPO validates the deliverable against the regulation directly.

---

## §5 Vendor stack (commercial layer, named)

| Layer | Vendor candidates | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Privacy management platform | OneTrust, TrustArc, BigID, Securiti.ai, Wire (Privacy Center), Osano | DSAR + consent + RoPA + DPIA + breach + transfer in one platform |
| Data discovery + classification | BigID, Securiti, Microsoft Purview, Collibra Protect, Privacera, Immuta | scan structured + unstructured stores for PII / SPI / PHI |
| Data catalog + lineage | Collibra, Alation, Informatica EDC, data.world, Atlan | RoPA + lineage backbone for DSAR scope-mapping |
| Consent management | OneTrust CMP, TrustArc CMP, Cookiebot (Usercentrics), Sourcepoint, Didomi | GDPR Art. 7 + CPRA opt-out symmetry |
| MDM | Informatica MDM, Reltio, Tibco EBX, Profisee, Stibo Systems | survivorship + minimization-aware merge rules |
| Data-quality | Informatica IDQ, Talend, Ataccama, Soda, Collibra DQ, Anomalo | rule-based + ML-based DQ |
| Breach + incident response | OneTrust Vendorpedia + Incident, Resilience, RadarFirst, BreachRX | breach-register + Art. 33 timer + multi-jurisdiction matrix |
| Cross-border transfer governance | OneTrust Trust Intelligence Cloud, TrustArc Privacy Profile, Tugboat Logic | SCC + BCR template management |

Per memory: the catalog stays vendor-agnostic at conformance grain; the vendor-stack table is buyer-facing context, not catalog content.

---

## §6 Render repos (OSS components for buy-vs-build sizing)

Drawn from `07_data_privacy_repos.tsv`. Stars + license + last-commit are the buy-vs-build signal.

| Workflow | Repo | Stars | License | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DSAR, consent, retention, DPIA, breach, transfer | ethyca/fides | 453 | Apache-2.0 | primary OSS privacy-engineering platform; DSAR + erasure orchestration; v2.84.0 |

| Data-quality | sodadata/soda-core | 2,300 | Elastic-2.0 | data-contract validation in YAML; license-flag — not OSI-approved |

| Retention | apache/atlas | 2,100 | Apache-2.0 | classification-driven retention enforcement; Hadoop-tier governance |
| DPIA | LINCnil/pia | 288 | GPL-3.0 | CNIL-published DPIA tool; the de-facto reference DPIA implementation |

License flags: AGPL-3.0 on zingg fires the buyer's IP review for SaaS/network-use scenarios; LGPL on dfir-iris + DataCleaner is permissive for use but copyleft on derivative-distribution; Confluent Community License + Elastic-2.0 are source-available but NOT OSI-approved (commercial-redistribution restrictions); GPL-3.0 on LINCnil/pia is copyleft on derivative-distribution. Permissive Apache-2.0 + MIT + BSD coverage is strong (great-expectations + buf + ethyca/fides + datahub + open-metadata + cookieconsent), supporting buy-vs-extend without IP friction across the most-cited workflows.

## §7 Cert paths the staffing stack draws from

| CIPP/E (Certified Information Privacy Professional / Europe) | IAPP | GDPR-program lead |

| CIPM (Certified Information Privacy Manager) | IAPP | privacy-program management; DPO-track |
| CIPT (Certified Information Privacy Technologist) | IAPP | privacy-by-design + privacy-engineering |

---

## §8 Authority closes (engagement-level, tier-ordered)

**Principle.** Privacy by Design (Cavoukian, 2009; recognized internationally as a foundational privacy principle and codified in GDPR Art. 25 as "data protection by design and by default"): privacy is engineered into systems from the outset rather than retrofitted; the seven foundational principles include proactive-not-reactive, privacy as default, end-to-end security, and full functionality without trade-offs. https://www.ipc.on.ca/wp-content/uploads/Resources/7foundationalprinciples.pdf, accessed 2026-04-28.

**Framework.** NIST Privacy Framework v1.0 — Identify-P + Govern-P + Control-P + Communicate-P + Protect-P functions organize the program structure; the catalog's data-privacy cluster maps cleanly to Identify-P (lineage + RoPA) + Control-P (consent + retention + DPIA) + Communicate-P (DSAR + breach notification) + Protect-P (security-of-processing). https://www.nist.gov/privacy-framework/privacy-framework, accessed 2026-04-27.

**Standard.** GDPR Articles 5, 7, 12–22, 25, 30, 32–34, 35, 44–49 + CCPA §1798.105 + .120 + ISO/IEC 27701:2019 PIMS, all verbatim in §4 above. https://gdpr-info.eu/, accessed 2026-04-27; https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/, accessed 2026-04-27; https://www.iso.org/standard/71670.html, accessed 2026-04-27.

---

## §9 What this walkthrough demonstrates about the catalog

1. **The catalog already knows this engagement.** 10 workflow profiles + 18 deliverables + 7 verbatim conformance citations + 18 OSS repos + 8 cert paths fall out of the existing substrate.
2. **GDPR is the verbatim authority spine; CCPA and US-state laws compose on top.** Of the 7 verbatim citations in §4, 5 are GDPR articles; CCPA appears as the downstream-propagation specialization that makes lineage non-optional. The catalog's privacy workflows are GDPR-anchored with CCPA + state-law overlays, matching the buyer-side legal-architecture reality.
3. **One platform (ethyca/fides) covers six of the ten workflows.** The OSS landscape concentrates orchestration in a single project (DSAR + consent + retention + DPIA + breach + transfer all flow through fides), with specialized projects for lineage (OpenLineage + DataHub + OpenMetadata) + DQ (Great Expectations + Soda) + DPIA (LINCnil/pia) + breach IR (dfir-iris). The catalog's workflow boundaries align with this OSS topology.
4. **The 72-hour Art. 33 timer is the program's keystone clock.** The breach-notification deliverable (D-72) anchors a chain back through detection (Workflow #9 + DevOps/SRE Workflow #1) + lineage (Workflow #3) + RoPA (D-64) + DPO communication (D-158); the 72-hour timer is what binds the chain operationally.

**Pattern-extension.** Same eight-section template completed across all four archetypes: Healthcare RCM at `applied_walkthrough_healthcare_rcm.md`, Public Sector ATO at `applied_walkthrough_public_sector_ato.md`, DevOps/SRE Reliability at `applied_walkthrough_devops_sre_reliability.md`, GDPR/CCPA Privacy at this file. The substrate at `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_workflows/clusters/` carries six more clusters ready for the same template (BSA/AML, Finance Close, Wealth Management, OT/Manufacturing, HR/Talent, Legal/Contract).

toolUseResult.content:
# Applied walkthrough — GDPR/CCPA Data Privacy Program

**Engagement archetype.** Mid-to-large organization (1,000–25,000 employees) installing or modernizing a privacy program: DSAR pipeline + consent management + data lineage + RoPA + DPIA workflow + retention enforcement + breach response + cross-border transfer governance. Could be a US-headquartered SaaS company building EU + UK + California compliance at once after a market-expansion trigger, a multinational re-baselining after the EU-US Data Privacy Framework + state-privacy-law expansion (Virginia + Colorado + Connecticut + Utah + Texas + Florida + Oregon + Iowa + Indiana + Tennessee + Montana), or a regulated-industry organization (healthcare, finance, ad-tech) layering privacy controls on top of an existing security program. DPO + Chief Privacy Officer + General Counsel as co-buyers; CISO + CDO consulted on data-discovery + breach-response legs. Indicative SOW: 12-week assessment + 24-week installation + ongoing managed-DPO retainer; 4–6 parallel workstreams; $400K–$1.5M for the build phase + $200K–$600K annual recurring for the managed leg.

---

## §1 Industry overlay (regulatory regimes binding from day one)

| Regime | Scope | Authority surface |
|---|---|---|
| GDPR (EU) | every workflow touching EU/EEA personal data | Articles 5 (principles) + 7 (consent) + 12–22 (data subject rights) + 25 (privacy by design) + 28 (processor) + 30 (RoPA) + 32–34 (security + breach) + 35 (DPIA) + 44–49 (transfers) |
| UK GDPR + Data Protection Act 2018 | UK personal data | UK GDPR mirrors EU GDPR with UK-specific schedules; ICO is the supervisory authority |
| CCPA / CPRA (California) | California consumer personal information | Cal. Civ. Code §1798.100 series; §1798.105 (deletion), §1798.120 (opt-out of sale/share) |
| US state privacy laws | residents of VA, CO, CT, UT, TX, FL, OR, IA, IN, TN, MT, OH, NJ, DE, NH, NE, KY, RI, MN, MD | varying scope, opt-out + sensitive-data + DPIA-equivalent + universal opt-out; each enacted 2023–2026 |
| HIPAA + HITECH | healthcare-overlapping use cases | breach notification overlaps with GDPR Art. 33 timing |
| EU SCC (Implementing Decision 2021/914) | cross-border transfers EU → third countries | modernized SCC modules; controller-to-controller, controller-to-processor, processor-to-processor |
| NIST Privacy Framework v1.0 | program structure | Identify-P + Govern-P + Control-P + Communicate-P + Protect-P functions |
| ISO/IEC 27701:2019 | PIMS extension to ISO 27001 | privacy information management system; certifiable atop ISO 27001 |
| DAMA-DMBOK 2 | data management knowledge | Ch 5 Data Modeling, Ch 10 Reference + Master Data, Ch 11 Data Warehousing + Lineage, Ch 13 Data Quality |

JD-corpus signal: privacy roles cluster around DSAR + GDPR + CCPA + DPO + privacy-engineering + data-governance language; cross-industry overlay (every industry ≥ certain size triggers privacy program).

---

## §2 Workflow profiles in scope (10 from the catalog's Data Privacy cluster)

| # | Workflow | What it does | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GDPR DSAR processing | Data Subject Access Request per GDPR Articles 12–15 + CCPA §1798.105 | P34 + P14 binding |
| 2 | Consent renewal cycle | GDPR consent re-collection + withdrawal handling + symmetric-withdrawal compliance | P34 + P32 binding |
| 3 | Data lineage discovery | automated + manual data-lineage discovery via metadata harvest | P01 + P34 binding |
| 4 | MDM cleanup cycle | Master Data Management deduplication + survivorship | P01 + P34 binding |
| 5 | Data quality issue resolution | DQ rule violation; investigation; fix; root cause | P22 + P01 binding |
| 6 | Schema evolution review | backward-compatible schema change per data contract | P27 + P29 binding |
| 7 | Data retention enforcement | retention-period enforcement + deletion per GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) | P34 + P14 binding |
| 8 | Privacy Impact Assessment (DPIA) | GDPR Article 35 DPIA for high-risk processing | P11 + P14 binding |
| 9 | Data breach notification | GDPR Article 33 72-hour notification to supervisory authority | P14.3 + P32 binding |
| 10 | Cross-border transfer review | GDPR Chapter V transfer mechanism (SCCs / BCRs / adequacy) | P14 + P08 binding |

Each profile carries verbatim conformance citations + recommended OSS render repos in `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_workflows/clusters/07_data_privacy_*.tsv`.

---

## §3 Deliverables produced (18 catalog items across A → H phases)

Grouped by TOGAF ADM phase. Deliverable IDs reference the catalog's master deliverable register.

**Phase 0 — Preliminary**
- D-40 Privacy Notice and Records Retention Schedule
- D-50 Data Governance Framework

**Phase B–D — Definition**
- D-19 Threat Model and Data Protection Impact Assessment
- D-64 Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) (GDPR Art. 30)
- D-98 Data Subject Access Request (DSAR) Procedure (GDPR Art. 12–22 + CCPA)
- D-108 Data Retention and Disposal Procedures (GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) + Art. 17)
- D-115 Quality Metrics Library (DQ rules + DPIA risk metrics)
- D-122 API Deprecation Policy (schema evolution governance)
- D-124 Cookie Policy and Consent Management (GDPR Art. 7 + ePrivacy + CPRA opt-out)
- D-131 Records Hold (Litigation Hold) Policy (overrides retention deletion under legal hold)
- D-151 Cross-Border Data Transfer Mechanism (SCC + BCR + adequacy decision)
- D-155 Data Lineage Documentation
- D-213 Data Catalog (PII + sensitive data classification)
- D-215 MDM Strategy (survivorship under accuracy + minimization principles)

**Phase F — Migration**
- D-72 Breach Notification Plan (GDPR Art. 33 72-hour timer + state-AG matrix)
- D-158 Incident Communication Plan (DPO + supervisory-authority + data-subject comms)

**Phase G — Implementation governance**
- + 2 supporting deliverables (IDs 63, 121) attached at workflow grain

Total: **18 distinct deliverables**, each with template + conformance acceptance criteria + a buyer's-eye signoff checklist.

---

## §4 Conformance contracts (verbatim, with URL + accessed-at)

A representative slice of the 10-workflow conformance set. The full register lives at `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_workflows/clusters/07_data_privacy_conformance.tsv`.

**DSAR — GDPR Art. 12(3) one-month timer.**
> "The controller shall provide information on action taken on a request under Articles 15 to 22 to the data subject without undue delay and in any event within one month of receipt of the request."
> https://gdpr-info.eu/art-12-gdpr/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. Anchors the DSAR pipeline's SLA design.

**DSAR — CCPA §1798.105(c)(1) downstream propagation.**
> "A business that receives a verifiable consumer request from a consumer to delete the consumer's personal information... shall delete the consumer's personal information from its records, notify any service providers or contractors to delete... and notify all third parties to whom the business has sold or shared the personal information to delete"
> https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=CIV&sectionNum=1798.105., accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. The downstream-propagation obligation is what makes lineage + processor-tracking non-optional.

**Consent — GDPR Art. 7(3) symmetric withdrawal.**
> "The data subject shall have the right to withdraw his or her consent at any time. The withdrawal of consent shall not affect the lawfulness of processing based on consent before its withdrawal. Prior to giving consent, the data subject shall be informed thereof. It shall be as easy to withdraw as to give consent."
> https://gdpr-info.eu/art-7-gdpr/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. The "as easy to withdraw as to give" constraint dictates UX symmetry in the consent-management module.

**Breach notification — GDPR Art. 33(1) 72-hour timer.**
> "The controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority"
> https://gdpr-info.eu/art-33-gdpr/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. The 72-hour timer is the program's most-cited regulatory clock.

**DPIA — GDPR Art. 35(7) minimum content.**
> "The assessment shall contain at least: a systematic description of the envisaged processing operations and the purposes... an assessment of the necessity and proportionality... an assessment of the risks to the rights and freedoms of data subjects... and the measures envisaged to address the risks, including safeguards, security measures and mechanisms"
> https://gdpr-info.eu/art-35-gdpr/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. Defines the DPIA template's required sections.

**Cross-border transfer — GDPR Art. 46(2) safeguards enumeration.**
> "The appropriate safeguards referred to in paragraph 1 may be provided for, without requiring any specific authorisation from a supervisory authority, by: a legally binding and enforceable instrument between public authorities or bodies; binding corporate rules in accordance with Article 47; standard data protection clauses adopted by the Commission..."
> https://gdpr-info.eu/art-46-gdpr/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. The decision tree for cross-border transfer review derives from this enumeration.

**RoPA — GDPR Art. 30(1).**
> "Each controller and, where applicable, the controller's representative, shall maintain a record of processing activities under its responsibility."
> https://gdpr-info.eu/art-30-gdpr/, accessed 2026-04-27.
> Tier: standard. The RoPA is the lineage-anchor obligation that data-discovery feeds.

Pattern: every privacy deliverable's acceptance criteria close on at least one verbatim citation from GDPR + CCPA + the NIST Privacy Framework + ISO/IEC 27701 + DAMA-DMBOK. The DPO validates the deliverable against the regulation directly.

---

## §5 Vendor stack (commercial layer, named)

| Layer | Vendor candidates | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Privacy management platform | OneTrust, TrustArc, BigID, Securiti.ai, Wire (Privacy Center), Osano | DSAR + consent + RoPA + DPIA + breach + transfer in one platform |
| Data discovery + classification | BigID, Securiti, Microsoft Purview, Collibra Protect, Privacera, Immuta | scan structured + unstructured stores for PII / SPI / PHI |
| Data catalog + lineage | Collibra, Alation, Informatica EDC, data.world, Atlan | RoPA + lineage backbone for DSAR scope-mapping |
| Consent management | OneTrust CMP, TrustArc CMP, Cookiebot (Usercentrics), Sourcepoint, Didomi | GDPR Art. 7 + CPRA opt-out symmetry |
| MDM | Informatica MDM, Reltio, Tibco EBX, Profisee, Stibo Systems | survivorship + minimization-aware merge rules |
| Data-quality | Informatica IDQ, Talend, Ataccama, Soda, Collibra DQ, Anomalo | rule-based + ML-based DQ |
| Breach + incident response | OneTrust Vendorpedia + Incident, Resilience, RadarFirst, BreachRX | breach-register + Art. 33 timer + multi-jurisdiction matrix |
| Cross-border transfer governance | OneTrust Trust Intelligence Cloud, TrustArc Privacy Profile, Tugboat Logic | SCC + BCR template management |

Per memory: the catalog stays vendor-agnostic at conformance grain; the vendor-stack table is buyer-facing context, not catalog content.

---

## §6 Render repos (OSS components for buy-vs-build sizing)

Drawn from `07_data_privacy_repos.tsv`. Stars + license + last-commit are the buy-vs-build signal.

| Workflow | Repo | Stars | License | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DSAR, consent, retention, DPIA, breach, transfer | ethyca/fides | 453 | Apache-2.0 | primary OSS privacy-engineering platform; DSAR + erasure orchestration; v2.84.0 |
| DSAR | opengdpr/OpenDSR | 386 | Apache-2.0 | cross-org DSR specification + reference flow |
| DSAR, lineage, retention, breach, transfer | datahub-project/datahub | 11,800 | Apache-2.0 | data catalog + column-level lineage; PII classification |
| Consent | orestbida/cookieconsent | 5,400 | MIT | lightweight cookie-consent UI + ledger; symmetric withdrawal |
| Consent | kiprotect/klaro | 1,400 | BSD-3-Clause | per-app + per-purpose consent UX |
| Lineage | OpenLineage/OpenLineage | 2,400 | Apache-2.0 | canonical lineage spec consumed by Marquez / DataHub |
| Lineage | MarquezProject/marquez | 2,200 | Apache-2.0 | OpenLineage reference backend |
| Lineage, schema-evolution, DPIA, transfer | open-metadata/OpenMetadata | 13,700 | Apache-2.0 | unified metadata platform; column-level lineage + PII tagging |
| MDM | zinggAI/zingg | 1,200 | AGPL-3.0 | ML-based dedupe + survivorship; AGPL fires source-disclosure on network use |
| MDM, DQ | datacleaner/DataCleaner | 650 | LGPL-3.0 | profiling + dedupe + matching + merging (Java); maintained-but-slowing |
| Data-quality | great-expectations/great_expectations | 11,400 | Apache-2.0 | declarative DQ rules + automated docs |
| Data-quality | sodadata/soda-core | 2,300 | Elastic-2.0 | data-contract validation in YAML; license-flag — not OSI-approved |
| Schema evolution | bufbuild/buf | 11,100 | Apache-2.0 | Protocol Buffers tooling; `buf breaking` enforces backward-compat |
| Schema evolution | confluentinc/schema-registry | 2,400 | Confluent-Community-License | source-available-NOT-OSI; client + Avro libs are Apache-2.0 |
| Schema evolution | Apicurio/apicurio-registry | 799 | Apache-2.0 | pure Apache-2.0 alternative to Confluent SR |
| Retention | apache/atlas | 2,100 | Apache-2.0 | classification-driven retention enforcement; Hadoop-tier governance |
| DPIA | LINCnil/pia | 288 | GPL-3.0 | CNIL-published DPIA tool; the de-facto reference DPIA implementation |
| Breach | dfir-iris/iris-web | 1,500 | LGPL-3.0 | case-management for the IR side of breach handling |

License flags: AGPL-3.0 on zingg fires the buyer's IP review for SaaS/network-use scenarios; LGPL on dfir-iris + DataCleaner is permissive for use but copyleft on derivative-distribution; Confluent Community License + Elastic-2.0 are source-available but NOT OSI-approved (commercial-redistribution restrictions); GPL-3.0 on LINCnil/pia is copyleft on derivative-distribution. Permissive Apache-2.0 + MIT + BSD coverage is strong (great-expectations + buf + ethyca/fides + datahub + open-metadata + cookieconsent), supporting buy-vs-extend without IP friction across the most-cited workflows.

---

## §7 Cert paths the staffing stack draws from

| Cert | Authority | Workflow scope |
|---|---|---|
| CIPP/E (Certified Information Privacy Professional / Europe) | IAPP | GDPR-program lead |
| CIPP/US | IAPP | US federal + state privacy law (CCPA + sectoral) |
| CIPM (Certified Information Privacy Manager) | IAPP | privacy-program management; DPO-track |
| CIPT (Certified Information Privacy Technologist) | IAPP | privacy-by-design + privacy-engineering |
| ISO/IEC 27701 Lead Implementer / Lead Auditor | PECB / Exemplar Global | PIMS implementation + audit |
| BCS Practitioner Certificate in Data Protection | BCS | UK GDPR + DPA 2018 |
| FIP (Fellow of Information Privacy) | IAPP | senior-level designation; CIPP + (CIPM or CIPT) prerequisite |
| CDPSE (Certified Data Privacy Solutions Engineer) | ISACA | technical privacy implementation |

---

## §8 Authority closes (engagement-level, tier-ordered)

**Principle.** Privacy by Design (Cavoukian, 2009; recognized internationally as a foundational privacy principle and codified in GDPR Art. 25 as "data protection by design and by default"): privacy is engineered into systems from the outset rather than retrofitted; the seven foundational principles include proactive-not-reactive, privacy as default, end-to-end security, and full functionality without trade-offs. https://www.ipc.on.ca/wp-content/uploads/Resources/7foundationalprinciples.pdf, accessed 2026-04-28.

**Framework.** NIST Privacy Framework v1.0 — Identify-P + Govern-P + Control-P + Communicate-P + Protect-P functions organize the program structure; the catalog's data-privacy cluster maps cleanly to Identify-P (lineage + RoPA) + Control-P (consent + retention + DPIA) + Communicate-P (DSAR + breach notification) + Protect-P (security-of-processing). https://www.nist.gov/privacy-framework/privacy-framework, accessed 2026-04-27.

**Standard.** GDPR Articles 5, 7, 12–22, 25, 30, 32–34, 35, 44–49 + CCPA §1798.105 + .120 + ISO/IEC 27701:2019 PIMS, all verbatim in §4 above. https://gdpr-info.eu/, accessed 2026-04-27; https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/, accessed 2026-04-27; https://www.iso.org/standard/71670.html, accessed 2026-04-27.

---

## §9 What this walkthrough demonstrates about the catalog

1. **The catalog already knows this engagement.** 10 workflow profiles + 18 deliverables + 7 verbatim conformance citations + 18 OSS repos + 8 cert paths fall out of the existing substrate.
2. **GDPR is the verbatim authority spine; CCPA and US-state laws compose on top.** Of the 7 verbatim citations in §4, 5 are GDPR articles; CCPA appears as the downstream-propagation specialization that makes lineage non-optional. The catalog's privacy workflows are GDPR-anchored with CCPA + state-law overlays, matching the buyer-side legal-architecture reality.
3. **One platform (ethyca/fides) covers six of the ten workflows.** The OSS landscape concentrates orchestration in a single project (DSAR + consent + retention + DPIA + breach + transfer all flow through fides), with specialized projects for lineage (OpenLineage + DataHub + OpenMetadata) + DQ (Great Expectations + Soda) + DPIA (LINCnil/pia) + breach IR (dfir-iris). The catalog's workflow boundaries align with this OSS topology.
4. **The 72-hour Art. 33 timer is the program's keystone clock.** The breach-notification deliverable (D-72) anchors a chain back through detection (Workflow #9 + DevOps/SRE Workflow #1) + lineage (Workflow #3) + RoPA (D-64) + DPO communication (D-158); the 72-hour timer is what binds the chain operationally.

**Pattern-extension.** Same eight-section template completed across all four archetypes: Healthcare RCM at `applied_walkthrough_healthcare_rcm.md`, Public Sector ATO at `applied_walkthrough_public_sector_ato.md`, DevOps/SRE Reliability at `applied_walkthrough_devops_sre_reliability.md`, GDPR/CCPA Privacy at this file. The substrate at `data/job_apps_sync/2026-04-27/industry_workflows/clusters/` carries six more clusters ready for the same template (BSA/AML, Finance Close, Wealth Management, OT/Manufacturing, HR/Talent, Legal/Contract).

1	# Applied walkthrough — Public Sector ATO Authorization Package
2	
3	**Engagement archetype.** Federal civilian agency, DoD program office, or cleared defense contractor pursuing Authority to Operate (ATO) for a moderate- or high-impact information system. Could be a greenfield FedRAMP authorization for a SaaS offering, a CMMC Level 2 or Level 3 readiness for a CUI-handling DIB contractor, an eMASS package preparation for a DoD IT system, or an ICD 503 / JSIG cycle for an Intelligence Community or SAP system. Authorizing Official + System Owner + ISSM as co-buyers; CIO + CISO consulted on enterprise risk acceptance. Indicative SOW: 6–12 month assessment + remediation cycle; 4–6 parallel workstreams; $750K–$3M depending on baseline (Moderate vs. High vs. CMMC L3 vs. SAP) and scope of remediation engineering.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## §1 Industry overlay (regulatory regimes binding from day one)
8	
9	| Regime | Scope | Authority surface |
10	|---|---|---|
11	| NIST RMF | every ATO workflow | NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2 — six-step lifecycle: Prepare, Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, Monitor |
12	| Control catalog | every system | NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 — security + privacy control catalog; FedRAMP Low/Moderate/High baselines derive from this |
13	| Assessment procedures | 3PAO + DIBCAC + IC assessors | NIST SP 800-53A Rev. 5 — assessment procedures matching the catalog |
14	| CUI protection | DIB contractors | NIST SP 800-171 Rev. 2 (110 controls, CMMC L2) + NIST SP 800-172 (enhanced, CMMC L3) |
15	| CMMC | DoD-prime + sub-tier contractors | 32 CFR § 170 — Final Rule; § 170.17 (L2 status types + 180-day POA&M), § 170.18 (DIBCAC L3 cycle) |
16	| FedRAMP | cloud service providers serving federal agencies | FedRAMP.gov Marketplace; Rev. 5 baselines aligned to NIST 800-53 Rev. 5; 3PAO assessment |
17	| DISA STIG | DoD-attached + cleared environments | XCCDF benchmarks per product/version on cyber.mil; CC SRG IL2/IL4/IL5/IL6 impact levels |
18	| ICD 503 | Intelligence Community systems | IC-CIO authority; references NIST + CNSSI 1253 categorization for NSS |
19	| JSIG | DoD Special Access Programs | JSIG = NIST 800-53 + CNSSI 1253 supplement under SAP AO; DoDM 5205.07 V1 umbrella |

1	# Applied walkthrough — DevOps/SRE Reliability Engineering Engagement
2	
3	**Engagement archetype.** Mid-to-large software organization (50–500 engineers across 5–30 services) standing up or modernizing the reliability practice: SLOs + error budgets + on-call + incident response + post-incident review + change management + release pipeline + chaos engineering + DR. Could be a SaaS company maturing from "ops by heroics" to a measured SRE practice, an enterprise consolidating fragmented incident-response across business units, or a regulated-industry IT org installing ITIL 4 Change Enablement on top of an existing CI/CD stack. VP Engineering + Director of Platform + CTO as co-buyers; CISO consulted on incident-response leg. Indicative SOW: 12-week assessment + 16-week installation + 12-week measurement; 5–7 parallel workstreams; $500K–$1.5M depending on scope of platform-team enablement + tooling integration.
4	
5	---
6

8	
9	| Framework / Standard | Scope | Authority surface |
10	|---|---|---|
11	| NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 | incident response lifecycle | four-phase: Preparation, Detection + Analysis, Containment + Eradication + Recovery, Post-Incident Activity (Rev. 2 withdrawn 2025-04-03; Rev. 3 supersedes; Rev. 2 phrasing remains canonical in industry use) |
12	| ITIL 4 Change Enablement (P27) | change management | RFC + CAB + change types (Standard / Normal / Emergency); change authority tiering |
13	| Google SRE Book + Workbook | SLO / error-budget / on-call / postmortem | Ch 3 Embracing Risk, Ch 5 Alerting on SLOs (Workbook), Ch 11 Being On-Call, Ch 14 Managing Incidents, Ch 15 Postmortem Culture, Ch 9 Incident Response (Workbook) |
14	| DORA Four Keys | release-cycle measurement | deployment frequency + change lead time + change failure rate + failed deployment recovery time |
15	| Martin Fowler bliki | release patterns | canary release + blue-green deployment + feature toggles canonical definitions |
16	| FIRST.org CVSS v3.1 + v4.0 | vulnerability triage | base + temporal + environmental + supplemental (v4.0) metrics; severity rating scale 0.0–10.0 |
17	| NIST SP 800-40 Rev. 4 | enterprise patch management | identify + prioritize + acquire + install + verify lifecycle |
18	| Principles of Chaos Engineering | chaos experiments | steady state + hypothesis + real-world variables + minimize blast radius |
19	| NIST CSRC glossary | DR + tabletop terminology | disaster recovery plan + tabletop exercise per CNSSI 4009-2015 / SP 800-34 / SP 800-84 |

1	# Applied walkthrough — GDPR/CCPA Data Privacy Program
2	
3	**Engagement archetype.** Mid-to-large organization (1,000–25,000 employees) installing or modernizing a privacy program: DSAR pipeline + consent management + data lineage + RoPA + DPIA workflow + retention enforcement + breach response + cross-border transfer governance. Could be a US-headquartered SaaS company building EU + UK + California compliance at once after a market-expansion trigger, a multinational re-baselining after the EU-US Data Privacy Framework + state-privacy-law expansion (Virginia + Colorado + Connecticut + Utah + Texas + Florida + Oregon + Iowa + Indiana + Tennessee + Montana), or a regulated-industry organization (healthcare, finance, ad-tech) layering privacy controls on top of an existing security program. DPO + Chief Privacy Officer + General Counsel as co-buyers; CISO + CDO consulted on data-discovery + breach-response legs. Indicative SOW: 12-week assessment + 24-week installation + ongoing managed-DPO retainer; 4–6 parallel workstreams; $400K–$1.5M for the build phase + $200K–$600K annual recurring for the managed leg.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## §1 Industry overlay (regulatory regimes binding from day one)
8	
9	| Regime | Scope | Authority surface |
10	|---|---|---|
11	| GDPR (EU) | every workflow touching EU/EEA personal data | Articles 5 (principles) + 7 (consent) + 12–22 (data subject rights) + 25 (privacy by design) + 28 (processor) + 30 (RoPA) + 32–34 (security + breach) + 35 (DPIA) + 44–49 (transfers) |
12	| UK GDPR + Data Protection Act 2018 | UK personal data | UK GDPR mirrors EU GDPR with UK-specific schedules; ICO is the supervisory authority |
13	| CCPA / CPRA (California) | California consumer personal information | Cal. Civ. Code §1798.100 series; §1798.105 (deletion), §1798.120 (opt-out of sale/share) |
14	| US state privacy laws | residents of VA, CO, CT, UT, TX, FL, OR, IA, IN, TN, MT, OH, NJ, DE, NH, NE, KY, RI, MN, MD | varying scope, opt-out + sensitive-data + DPIA-equivalent + universal opt-out; each enacted 2023–2026 |
15	| HIPAA + HITECH | healthcare-overlapping use cases | breach notification overlaps with GDPR Art. 33 timing |
16	| EU SCC (Implementing Decision 2021/914) | cross-border transfers EU → third countries | modernized SCC modules; controller-to-controller, controller-to-processor, processor-to-processor |
17	| NIST Privacy Framework v1.0 | program structure | Identify-P + Govern-P + Control-P + Communicate-P + Protect-P functions |
18	| ISO/IEC 27701:2019 | PIMS extension to ISO 27001 | privacy information management system; certifiable atop ISO 27001 |
19	| DAMA-DMBOK 2 | data management knowledge | Ch 5 Data Modeling, Ch 10 Reference + Master Data, Ch 11 Data Warehousing + Lineage, Ch 13 Data Quality |
20	
21	JD-corpus signal: privacy roles cluster around DSAR + GDPR + CCPA + DPO + privacy-engineering + data-governance language; cross-industry overlay (every industry ≥ certain size triggers privacy program).
